concise:
Ayah 1–4 · linked video: 01. Al-Isra’ (Ayah 1-4)

Surah Al-Isra Commentary: Ayahs 1-4

Ayah 1

سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَسْرَىٰ بِعَبْدِهِۦ لَيْلًا مِّنَ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ إِلَى ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْأَقْصَا ٱلَّذِى بَـٰرَكْنَا حَوْلَهُۥ لِنُرِيَهُۥ مِنْ ءَايَـٰتِنَآ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْبَصِيرُ

As we reach Surah Al-Isra, we encounter one of the most central chapters of the Quran. Along with Surah Al-Kahf that follows it, these surahs contain some of the deepest aspects of Quranic wisdom. They introduce themes that haven’t been touched upon before in the Quran and elaborate on concepts that Allah ﷻ had only briefly mentioned earlier. These two surahs are beautifully interlocked with each other in theme, structure and style.

Surah Al-Isra, also known as Surah Bani Israel, has distinct qualities. Most notably, it presents a comprehensive summary of Jewish history spanning approximately 2,000 years, all condensed into just four ayahs. The entire career of Bani Israel went through four major phases, and all four are highlighted here. This raises an important question: why does Allah ﷻ mention this history at the beginning of the surah?

As we study further, we discover that Jewish history is actually inseparable from Muslim history. The Prophet ﷺ compared the two nations, saying: “The same things that befell Bani Israel will fall upon my ummah, like one shoe compared to another.” This comparison is profound. When shoes are on the ground, you can easily tell left from right. But when placed next to each other, they line up perfectly. There’s wisdom in this parallel because while two things can be very similar, shoes have a peculiar quality - they’re made the same size for the same person, but one is meant for the right foot and the other for the left. So while there are many parallels between the two nations, there are also distinctive qualities. One went a certain way, while the other is meant to follow a different path.

The first event mentioned in this surah is directly tied to Jewish history. Allah ﷻ opens with: سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَسْرَىٰ بِعَبْدِهِۦ لَيْلًا - “How perfect is the One who took His slave in the middle of the night.”

The word أَسْرَىٰ itself means to take someone at night. The root word سُرَىٰ means night, so asra means to take at night. We’ve seen this word before in the Quran: أَسْرِ بِأَهْلِكَ بِقِطْعٍ مِّنَ ٱلَّيْلِ - “Take your family and hurry them along in a piece of the night.”

When Allah ﷻ says أَسْرَىٰ بِعَبْدِهِۦ لَيْلًا, He’s making a specific linguistic choice. The word asra by itself already means to take at night. But then Allah ﷻ adds لَيْلًا. If He had said لَيْلَةً, meaning “a single night,” it would still imply the entire night. However, laylan allows us to understand this as a very small piece of the night. This journey took only a tiny portion of the night.

This ayah refers to the first part of a two-part journey: Al-Isra wal-Mi’raj - the Night Journey and the Ascension. This surah mentions only the night journey. The ascension to the heavens is mentioned in Surah An-Najm. Here, we read about the Prophet’s ﷺ journey to Jerusalem.

The ayah continues: مِّنَ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ إِلَى ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْأَقْصَا - “from Al-Masjid Al-Haram to Al-Masjid Al-Aqsa.” The Prophet ﷺ had already been declared by the Quran as the imam of Makkah, and now he was going to be declared the imam of Al-Aqsa as well. One of the most important events during this journey was that the Messenger ﷺ led all the other prophets in prayer. He became the imam of both Makkah and Al-Aqsa, and the imam of all previous prophets.

Allah ﷻ describes Al-Aqsa as ٱلَّذِى بَـٰرَكْنَا حَوْلَهُۥ - “the one whose surroundings We had made full of blessing.” This has a literal meaning - the land of Jerusalem is very lush, green and beautiful, full of vegetation. But additionally, Allah ﷻ blessed it with prophets and revelation. It is one of the places where Ibrahim (AS) set foot. Ibrahim (AS) left one of his sons there and the other in Makkah. The son left in the Jerusalem region was Ishaq (AS), and his legacy continued through Ya’qub (AS) and his descendants. The other son, Ismail (AS), was left in Makkah, and from his lineage came the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. Now, one of the descendants from these two legacies was going to unify them - becoming the imam of both Al-Haram and Al-Aqsa together.

Allah ﷻ explains the purpose of this journey: لِنُرِيَهُۥ مِنْ ءَايَـٰتِنَآ - “in order to show him some of Our miraculous signs.” This incredible event happened in the blink of an eye. According to some authorities, the door latch was still swinging when the Prophet ﷺ returned - that’s how quick this occurred in our time, though time in the realm of the Unseen operates differently.

When the Prophet ﷺ informed people about what had occurred, the Quraysh were overjoyed. They had been calling him insane all this time, and now they had something to use against his followers. They rushed to Abu Bakr (RA) and said, “Do you know what your prophet just said? He claims he went to Jerusalem and came back all in one part of the night!”

Abu Bakr (RA) responded, “Did he really say that?”

They said, “Yes, he said it. If you want, you can come with us and he can tell you himself.”

He replied, “If he said it, it happened.”

That was his conviction. If his mind couldn’t wrap itself around it, that wasn’t a problem anymore. He had already accepted Allah’s ﷻ Messenger as the true Messenger.

The ayah concludes: إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْبَصِيرُ - “No doubt, He is the All-Hearing, the All-Seeing.” Notice how Allah ﷻ describes His Messenger at this highest point - at the pinnacle of human achievement, traveling to the heavens - with the term عَبْدِهِۦ (His slave). We understand from this that the lowest standing we have before Allah ﷻ, the standing of being His slave, is actually the highest standing. There is no greater honor than humbling yourself before Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 2

وَءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَجَعَلْنَـٰهُ هُدًى لِّبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ أَلَّا تَتَّخِذُوا۟ مِن دُونِى وَكِيلًا

Now that Jerusalem has been mentioned, Allah ﷻ begins to discuss the main legacy of Jerusalem, the primary legacy of Al-Masjid Al-Aqsa. At the center of this legacy is Musa (AS), the one who revived this heritage, who brought his people out of slavery and the clutches of Firaun, leading them to this blessed land.

Allah ﷻ says: وَءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ - “And We gave Musa the Book.” Then He says: وَجَعَلْنَـٰهُ هُدًى لِّبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ - “And We made it a guide for the Children of Israel.” Notice the specificity. About the Quran, Allah ﷻ says هُدًى لِّلنَّاسِ - “guidance for all people.” But about the book given to Musa (AS), He says it was guidance specifically for Bani Israel. It wasn’t meant for everyone.

Then comes something beautiful: أَلَّا تَتَّخِذُوا۟ مِن دُونِى وَكِيلًا - “that you shouldn’t take anyone as a wakil other than Me, besides Myself.” This happens sometimes in the Quran - Allah ﷻ reveals something that contains numerous pieces of wisdom, many aspects of belief, countless commands and prohibitions. But sometimes He highlights just one thing and says: if you held onto this one thing, everything else would make sense. That’s what He does here. The summary of the Torah, the summary of all Mosaic revelation, condensed into one statement: don’t take anyone to rely upon absolutely except Myself.

The word وَكِيلًا is profound. Wakil means to put your trust in someone with a personal matter - something you really need to get done yourself, something you wouldn’t normally trust with anybody else. Maybe it’s delivering an important message, or taking care of something inside your house, or resolving a family dispute. It’s something very private. You don’t want to get outsiders involved, but when you trust someone to the point that even though they’re an outsider, you bring them into that personal matter and rely on them completely - that’s wakil. It goes even further: you not only rely on them and bring them in on the matter, you leave the whole thing to them. Whatever they decide to do, they do it at their discretion. That kind of trust and reliance - that’s wakil.

Allah ﷻ says the summary of revelation to Bani Israel was this: don’t take anyone to rely upon absolutely except Me.

Ayah 3

ذُرِّيَّةَ مَنْ حَمَلْنَا مَعَ نُوحٍ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ عَبْدًا شَكُورًا

This ayah creates a beautiful connection. Allah ﷻ addresses Bani Israel: ذُرِّيَّةَ مَنْ حَمَلْنَا مَعَ نُوحٍ - “The offspring of those We boarded along with Nuh.”

It’s actually quite remarkable when you think about it. How did the nation of Musa (AS) end up in the Promised Land? Eventually they crossed water, didn’t they? When they escaped from Firaun, they crossed the sea. But this isn’t the first time in their ancestry that water was crossed to save their people. Way before Musa (AS), far back in their lineage, there was another rescue involving water - the flood during the time of Nuh (AS).

There’s a parallel here. When Jews think of rescue, they think of Musa (AS) parting the sea. But the first rescue was actually that of Nuh (AS) and those who boarded the ark with him. Bani Israel are actually descendants of Nuh (AS). From his three sons - Sam, Ham and Yafith - it is from Sam that the lineage continues down to Ibrahim (AS) and then to the Israelites through Ishaq and Ya’qub (AS).

Then Allah ﷻ says: إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ عَبْدًا شَكُورًا - “No doubt, he had been an extremely, excessively grateful slave.” When you’re told about your father that he was extremely grateful, what’s being implied between the lines? You should be too.

If someone comes to you and says, “You know, your dad was very respectful,” what are they really saying? They’re implying, “What’s wrong with you?” It’s actually an indirect criticism. When Allah ﷻ tells Bani Israel, “Your forefather Nuh was an extremely grateful slave,” there’s a pause after that - a dot, dot, dot. The implication is clear: he was excessively grateful, so what happened to you?

This is a Makkan surah, so what’s happening here is fascinating. The Israelites are being addressed indirectly because they’re posing their questions to the Prophet ﷺ through the Makkans. The Makkans are acting as intermediaries, but the real audience - Bani Israel - are being spoken to through the text.

Ayah 4

وَقَضَيْنَآ إِلَىٰ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَتُفْسِدُنَّ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مَرَّتَيْنِ وَلَتَعْلُنَّ عُلُوًّا كَبِيرًا

Here begins the historical account of Bani Israel, presented with remarkable brevity. Allah ﷻ says: وَقَضَيْنَآ إِلَىٰ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ - “And We had declared to the Children of Israel in the Book.”

What was declared? لَتُفْسِدُنَّ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مَرَّتَيْنِ - “You are going to cause corruption in the land twice.” You’re going to cause a lot of trouble, a lot of mischief in the land. Note that to cause trouble in the land, you have to have some kind of power, some kind of authority. If you don’t have authority, you can’t cause widespread corruption. If you don’t have the means, you can’t create havoc. Simple as that.

Allah ﷻ specifies: مَرَّتَيْنِ - “two times.” You will do this twice. Then comes the powerful conclusion: وَلَتَعْلُنَّ عُلُوًّا كَبِيرًا - “And you will rise with tremendous arrogance.”

The word عُلُوًّا is fascinating. It comes from عَلا, which means to be high. But علا has two distinct meanings. First, it means to rise high, to gain great power, to reach great heights. Second, it means to raise your head in rebellion - the kind of defiance where someone says something and you respond with an attitude, raising your head in disobedience.

So when Allah ﷻ says وَلَتَعْلُنَّ عُلُوًّا كَبِيرًا, He’s saying two things simultaneously: You’re going to have huge amounts of power, and at the same time, your rebellion against Allah ﷻ is going to be immense. The word captures both meanings - tremendous worldly power and tremendous spiritual arrogance.

This was already declared in the Book about the legacy of Bani Israel: you will have two rises as a nation. You will experience two historical ascensions to power. And during these times of power, you will cause corruption in the land twice, and you will rise with tremendous arrogance twice - وَلَتَعْلُنَّ عُلُوًّا كَبِيرًا.

The stage is now set for understanding the cyclical pattern of Bani Israel’s history, their rises and falls, and the lessons these patterns hold not just for them, but for the Muslim ummah as well.

Ayah 4–5 · linked video: 02. Al-Isra’ (Ayah 4-5)

Surah Al-Isra: Ayahs 4-5

Ayah 4

وَقَضَيْنَآ إِلَىٰ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَتُفْسِدُنَّ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مَرَّتَيْنِ وَلَتَعْلُنَّ عُلُوًّا كَبِيرًا

And We decreed for the Children of Israel in the Scripture that you will surely cause corruption on the earth twice, and you will surely reach a great height of arrogance.

This ayah marks a pivotal moment in the Quran’s historical narrative. After the miraculous night journey of the Prophet ﷺ, Allah ﷻ immediately shifts our attention to a divine decree made to Bani Israel in their Scripture. The word قَضَيْنَآ carries profound implications—it means We decreed, We informed, We made known with absolute certainty. This wasn’t merely a warning or possibility; it was a guaranteed prophecy written in their own Book, the Torah itself.

Allah ﷻ informed them: لَتُفْسِدُنَّ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مَرَّتَيْنِ—you will surely cause corruption in the earth twice. The emphasis here is unmistakable. This corruption wouldn’t be minor or isolated; it would be widespread and severe enough to provoke divine intervention. Coupled with this is وَلَتَعْلُنَّ عُلُوًّا كَبِيرًا—you will reach a tremendous height of arrogance. The word عُلُوّ signifies not just pride, but an attitude of superiority that manifests in oppression and transgression against others.

To understand these two rises and falls, we must journey through the complex history of Bani Israel. After Musa (AS) received the Torah at Mount Sinai and passed away, a new generation was born in the desert—tougher, raised in hardship, and hungry for a better life. Under the leadership of Yusha’ ibn Nun, the successor to Musa (AS), they finally achieved victory and gained authority in the blessed land. But almost immediately, the unity fractured. The twelve tribes established twelve separate kingdoms, dividing what should have been one nation into competing states.

This internal division mirrors a pattern we see throughout history, including in our own Muslim Ummah today. When people are ethnically one, religiously one, and historically one, yet divide themselves along tribal or national lines, they become inherently weak. To outsiders, they appear as one people, but internally, they’re consumed by protecting their interests from their own brothers. This fragmentation makes them an easy target for external enemies.

The first significant invasion came from the Assyrians, who exploited these divisions. But the real catastrophe—the first major fall referenced in this ayah—came in 587 BCE at the hands of the Babylonians under Nebuchadnezzar. This wasn’t merely a military defeat; it was near-total destruction. Jerusalem was invaded, the Temple of Sulayman (AS) was demolished, 600,000 people were killed, and the remaining 600,000 were taken as slaves to Babylon. The city was left completely abandoned. This was the first bottom they hit after their first rise under Dawud (AS) and Sulayman (AS), who had ruled for approximately 100 years combined.

Then came a revival. The Persians defeated the Babylonians, and under their rule, Bani Israel were freed and allowed to return to their homeland. Some scholars even suggest that the Persian ruler Cyrus might have been Dhul-Qarnayn mentioned in the Quran, though this remains a matter of scholarly discussion. The second rise began—the Maccabean rule—which actually extended even further than Sulayman’s (AS) empire had reached.

But with affluence and power came the inevitable decline. When nations reach their peak, moral corruption begins to set in. They fell into luxury, shamelessness, addiction to entertainment, and all forms of transgression. Magic and spell-casting became prevalent. Their religion became more about cultural identity than about submission to Allah ﷻ. This weakness invited invasion once again.

The Greeks attacked, followed by the Romans. By the time Isa (AS) was born, Bani Israel were living under Roman occupation with puppet kings serving Roman interests. Then, approximately 70 years after Isa (AS), came the second catastrophic fall. The Romans launched a brutal assault on Jerusalem, killing 130,000 people in a single day. Historical accounts describe that not two bricks remained intact. The Temple was destroyed for the second time, and it remains in that destroyed state to this day.

This is the pattern Allah ﷻ had decreed and informed them about in their own Scripture: two major rises, two major falls. Two periods where they would hold power and reach great heights of arrogance, followed by two devastating collapses as consequences for their corruption.

The parallels to Islamic history are striking and should give us pause. Our first rise came with the establishment of the Islamic state in Madinah under the Prophet ﷺ and the Rightly Guided Caliphs (RA), extending through the Umayyad and Abbasid periods. Our first significant fall came with the Mongol invasions—the Tatars who massacred Muslims and made piles of scholars’ heads. Ironically, these same Tatars later accepted Islam. Our second rise came through the Turkish dynasties—the Ayyubids, Seljuks, Mamluks, and ultimately the Ottomans. Our second fall began with European colonization, which dismantled Muslim lands piece by piece, culminating in the dissolution of the last Caliphate in 1924.

We are now living in the aftermath of that second fall, just as Bani Israel lived for 500 years in diaspora before the coming of our Prophet ﷺ. These two histories—theirs and ours—are like two kites flying through time, and Allah ﷻ has decreed that their strings will eventually cross again when Isa (AS) returns.

Ayah 5

فَإِذَا جَآءَ وَعْدُ أُولَىٰهُمَا بَعَثْنَا عَلَيْكُمْ عِبَادًا لَّنَآ أُو۟لِى بَأْسٍ شَدِيدٍ فَجَاسُوا۟ خِلَـٰلَ ٱلدِّيَارِ ۚ وَكَانَ وَعْدًا مَّفْعُولًا

So when the promise of the first of them came, We sent against you servants of Ours possessing great military might, and they penetrated through the homes. And it was a promise fulfilled.

This ayah describes the execution of the divine decree. فَإِذَا جَآءَ وَعْدُ أُولَىٰهُمَا—when the promise of the first of the two came. Notice Allah ﷻ calls it a “promise” (وَعْد). When corruption and arrogance reach their peak, divine intervention becomes inevitable. This isn’t arbitrary punishment; it’s a natural consequence spelled out in advance.

بَعَثْنَا عَلَيْكُمْ عِبَادًا لَّنَآ—We sent against you servants of Ours. This phrase is remarkable. Allah ﷻ calls the Babylonian invaders “servants of Ours”—عِبَادًا لَّنَآ. They weren’t believers; they were actually pagans who worshipped idols. Yet Allah ﷻ claims ownership of them as His servants because, ultimately, everything in creation serves the will of Allah ﷻ, whether consciously or unconsciously. They were instruments of divine justice, fulfilling a role in Allah’s plan even without knowing it.

These servants were أُو۟لِى بَأْسٍ شَدِيدٍ—possessors of great military might. The word بَأْس refers to severe warfare capability, strength in battle, and the ability to inflict devastating military power. The Babylonians under Nebuchadnezzar were indeed a formidable force, and they brought that full might against Jerusalem.

Then comes the chilling description: فَجَاسُوا۟ خِلَـٰلَ ٱلدِّيَارِ—they penetrated through the homes. The word جَاسُوا۟ is incredibly specific. It means to forcefully invade and penetrate, to go into the tightest spaces, to leave no corner unsearched. Think of how a toothpick penetrates between teeth where the space is extremely tight—that’s the imagery of جَوس. They didn’t just conquer the city; they went door to door, house to house, room to room. No closet was too small, no hiding place too hidden.

The word خِلَـٰلَ reinforces this—it means “between” but specifically between things that are tightly packed together. Between the homes, through the homes, in every space between. This detail tells us something crucial: this wasn’t a battlefield war between armies. The war came to the city, into civilian areas, into people’s homes. The victims weren’t primarily soldiers but ordinary people—families in their houses, with soldiers busting down doors and invading their private spaces.

This level of detail serves multiple purposes. It shows the severity of the punishment—total, comprehensive, inescapable. It also serves as a historical marker so clear that when it happened, they would recognize it as the fulfillment of prophecy. There could be no doubt: this was the first of the two promises being fulfilled.

وَكَانَ وَعْدًا مَّفْعُولًا—and it was a promise fulfilled. The word مَّفْعُولًا emphasizes that it was actually done, completely executed, brought to full realization. Allah’s promises, whether of reward or consequence, are never empty threats. When He decrees something, it will come to pass exactly as described.

This ayah stands as both a historical record and a timeless warning. For Bani Israel, it was a reminder that their own Scripture had warned them, yet they proceeded down the path of corruption and arrogance anyway. For us as Muslims reading this passage, it’s a mirror held up to our own condition. We see the same patterns emerging in our history: unity giving way to division, religious commitment fading into cultural identity, moral strength crumbling under the weight of luxury and entertainment, and ultimately, external forces exploiting our weakness.

The lesson isn’t just about them; it’s about the universal principles of how nations rise and fall. When people abandon justice, spread corruption, and become arrogant in the earth, divine consequences follow as surely as night follows day. And when those consequences come, they come thoroughly—فَجَاسُوا۟ خِلَـٰلَ ٱلدِّيَارِ—penetrating every space, leaving nothing untouched. This is وَعْدًا مَّفْعُولًا—a promise that will be fulfilled.

Ayah 6–14 · linked video: 03. Al-Isra’ (Ayah 6-14)

Surah Al-Isra: Ayahs 6-14

Ayah 6

ثُمَّ رَدَدْنَا لَكُمُ ٱلْكَرَّةَ عَلَيْهِمْ وَأَمْدَدْنَـٰكُم بِأَمْوَٰلٍ وَبَنِينَ وَجَعَلْنَـٰكُمْ أَكْثَرَ نَفِيرًا

After the first punishment and subsequent humiliation, Allah ﷻ describes His mercy in allowing the Israelites a comeback. The phrase رَدَدْنَا لَكُمُ ٱلْكَرَّةَ means “We returned for you the turn” - in other words, We gave you another opportunity to rise again. This wasn’t simply a restoration but a divine reversal of their fortunes against their enemies.

The support Allah ﷻ provided wasn’t merely spiritual. He aided them وَأَمْدَدْنَـٰكُم بِأَمْوَٰلٍ وَبَنِينَ - with wealth and sons. These represent the fundamental building blocks of any civilization: economic strength and demographic power. But Allah ﷻ doesn’t stop at these essentials. He says وَجَعَلْنَـٰكُمْ أَكْثَرَ نَفِيرًا - We made you greater in manpower.

The word نَفِير specifically refers to a small battalion ready to mobilize and fight. Rather than using a general term for army, Allah ﷻ employs this particular word to emphasize military readiness and strength. The message is clear: We didn’t just give you numbers, We gave you organized, prepared military might. You became militarily powerful once again.

Ayah 7

إِنْ أَحْسَنتُمْ أَحْسَنتُمْ لِأَنفُسِكُمْ ۖ وَإِنْ أَسَأْتُمْ فَلَهَا ۚ فَإِذَا جَآءَ وَعْدُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ لِيَسُـۥٓـُٔوا۟ وُجُوهَكُمْ وَلِيَدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْمَسْجِدَ كَمَا دَخَلُوهُ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ وَلِيُتَبِّرُوا۟ مَا عَلَوْا۟ تَتْبِيرًا

Having been granted this tremendous comeback, the Israelites are reminded of a fundamental principle: إِنْ أَحْسَنتُمْ أَحْسَنتُمْ لِأَنفُسِكُمْ - if you do good, you’ve only done good for yourselves. When you excel, when you act righteously, the primary beneficiary is you. This isn’t charity toward Allah ﷻ; this is investment in your own future.

Then comes the sobering reality: وَإِنْ أَسَأْتُمْ فَلَهَا - and if you do wrong, then it is for it. Notice the remarkable shift in the Arabic structure. When speaking of good deeds, Allah ﷻ says لِأَنفُسِكُمْ - for yourselves, maintaining direct second-person address. But when mentioning wrongdoing, He shifts to فَلَهَا - for it, using the third person.

Why this grammatical distancing? Because wrongdoing is disappointing to Him. By switching to the pronoun “it,” Allah ﷻ creates linguistic separation, as though He doesn’t even want to directly associate them with their evil actions. Yet there’s another layer here: the pronoun technically still refers back to “yourselves,” but by making it third person, it’s as if their own souls have become something other - something they’ve turned against. The لَـ here can also indicate purpose or intent, suggesting: you did wrong thinking it would bring you benefit, you acted out of greed for yourselves.

Then comes the prophetic warning about the second corruption: فَإِذَا جَآءَ وَعْدُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ - when the promise of the latter came. This isn’t referring to the Day of Judgment, but rather to the second of the two promised punishments mentioned earlier. The first time they corrupted the land, an army entered every home. Now, when the final promise arrives, there’s actually an implied phrase here - the same structure from before is understood: We appointed another overwhelmingly powerful army against you.

لِيَسُـۥٓـُٔوا۟ وُجُوهَكُمْ - to darken your faces. This is an expression of public humiliation and disgrace. In Arab culture, to have one’s face darkened means to be utterly shamed before everyone.

وَلِيَدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْمَسْجِدَ كَمَا دَخَلُوهُ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ - and to enter the mosque just as they entered it the first time. History repeats itself with precision. Just as the Assyrians or Babylonians devastated Jerusalem and destroyed the Temple in the first corruption, the Romans under Titus would do the same in 70 CE, entering the sacred precinct and leaving no stone upon stone.

وَلِيُتَبِّرُوا۟ مَا عَلَوْا۟ تَتْبِيرًا - and to utterly destroy whatever they had elevated. The word تَتْبِير means complete and total annihilation, to destroy something so thoroughly that no trace remains. The root carries the meaning of reducing something to fragments, to pulverize it. Whatever civilization they had built, whatever heights they had reached, whatever structures they had elevated - all would be demolished so completely that no one would even know a civilization had existed there. Indeed, after the destruction in 70 CE, Jerusalem was abandoned as a city, its inhabitants killed or taken captive, the area left desolate.

Ayah 8

عَسَىٰ رَبُّكُمْ أَن يَرْحَمَكُمْ ۚ وَإِنْ عُدتُّمْ عُدْنَا ۘ وَجَعَلْنَا جَهَنَّمَ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ حَصِيرًا

After describing these devastating punishments, something remarkable happens in the discourse. Allah ﷻ begins speaking directly to the Israelites: عَسَىٰ رَبُّكُمْ أَن يَرْحَمَكُمْ - perhaps your Master will show you mercy. Notice that the conversation began indirectly with Bani Israel earlier in the surah, but once the direct second-person address began, it continues here. Allah ﷻ knows they’re listening, whether in the Prophet’s ﷺ time or throughout history.

This statement carries profound hope: it’s not that Allah ﷻ hates them, not that He put them through difficulty because they’re no longer qualified for His mercy. The door remains open. Maybe - just maybe - your Lord will still show you mercy despite everything.

But then comes the stern warning: وَإِنْ عُدتُّمْ عُدْنَا - but if you return, We will return. If you go back to your old ways, if you resort to your previous behavior, We’ll go back to Our old punishments. History will repeat itself. It happened twice and you were punished twice. You want to go back? We’ll do it again. The pattern is established, and the choice is yours.

وَجَعَلْنَا جَهَنَّمَ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ حَصِيرًا - and We have made Hell for the disbelievers as a prison facility. The word حَصِير refers to something that completely surrounds and imprisons. It’s not just punishment; it’s constant, inescapable confinement. For those who reject faith entirely, there awaits not temporary earthly punishment but eternal imprisonment in the Fire.

Ayah 9

إِنَّ هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ يَهْدِى لِلَّتِى هِىَ أَقْوَمُ وَيُبَشِّرُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ أَنَّ لَهُمْ أَجْرًا كَبِيرًا

After addressing the historical failings of the Israelites, Allah ﷻ presents the solution: إِنَّ هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ يَهْدِى لِلَّتِى هِىَ أَقْوَمُ - indeed, this Quran guides to that which is most upright. The way to set things right, the path forward, is this Quran.

The word أَقْوَمُ is comparative - “more upright,” “more correct,” “more proper.” If Allah ﷻ had said الْأَقْوَمُ - “the most upright” - it would be superlative without comparison. But by using أَقْوَمُ without the definite article, He establishes a comparison. Compared to what? By definition, this creates a contrast with the Torah and previous revelations. There may be some guidance in the old revelation, some wisdom still remaining in it, but this is better. This is the one guiding you to the more right way, the more correct path.

Yet the scope of this statement extends beyond just the Jews. Every one of us, Muslim or not, has a certain lifestyle, a philosophy of life. And in every lifestyle, every philosophy, there exists some wisdom. It’s not all based on foolishness. Even atheists have some wisdom in their approach to life. Even people following other traditions have insights worth noting. If you study their literature, you might find meaningful observations. That’s fine - Allah ﷻ acknowledges that. But He declares: the more correct way, the one without fundamental flaws, is this one. This is the guidance that doesn’t lead you astray.

وَيُبَشِّرُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ - and it gives good news to the believers who do righteous deeds. Notice that Allah ﷻ specifically highlights good deeds alongside belief. Why this emphasis? Because essentially, the problem with the Israelites was that they had resorted to claiming “belief is enough.” We believe, we’re already chosen, why should we be held accountable?

No - true believers are those who translate faith into action. أَنَّ لَهُمْ أَجْرًا كَبِيرًا - they will have enormous compensation, tremendous reward. The word كَبِير emphasizes magnitude, a reward so vast it defies easy description.

Ayah 10

وَأَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ أَعْتَدْنَا لَهُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا

After the good news comes the stern warning: وَأَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ أَعْتَدْنَا لَهُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا - and as for those who don’t believe in the afterlife, We have prepared for them a painful punishment.

The word أَعْتَدْنَا comes from the root عَتَادْ, which refers to bags that you pack when you’re going on a particular mission - packing specifically what you’ll need, the essentials. Not packing everything randomly, but preparing exactly what’s required for a specific purpose. When you prepare the essentials, when you pack precisely for something, that’s عَتَادْ. The common meaning became “to prepare,” but with the connotation of preparing specifically, deliberately, for a particular situation.

When Allah ﷻ says We have prepared a punishment particularly for them, it carries a chilling specificity. In a prison, you throw all kinds of criminals together for different crimes. But Allah ﷻ is saying: We’ve made a special arrangement for you. Your torture is very particular to your crimes. It’s been prepared specifically, deliberately, with your specific rejection in mind. عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا - a painful punishment that cuts deep.

Ayah 11

وَيَدْعُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ بِٱلشَّرِّ دُعَآءَهُۥ بِٱلْخَيْرِ ۖ وَكَانَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ عَجُولًا

This ayah reveals a profound truth about human nature: وَيَدْعُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ بِٱلشَّرِّ دُعَآءَهُۥ بِٱلْخَيْرِ - and the human being calls for evil as he calls for good. The structure is complex and revealing. What it actually means is: the human being calls for things thinking they’re good, but doesn’t realize he’s actually calling for evil. He has no idea what he’s truly asking for.

In the immediate historical context, some among the Quraysh were already making supplications against the Prophet ﷺ, thinking they were doing something righteous. “O Allah, there’s a prophet from among our own people - destroy him! He’s a false prophet!” They were rushing to judgment, calling down destruction without understanding what they were truly doing.

But the general meaning extends to all of us. Sometimes we don’t realize what we’re asking Allah ﷻ for. We become desperate: “I don’t care what happens, just get me this job. I don’t want anything else. Just give me this job.” But you don’t know if that job is good for you or not. You simply don’t know. The proper way to ask is: “O Allah, if this is good for me, facilitate it for me.” You can’t approach Him with certainty about what’s best for you. Especially when it comes to future matters, you must ask based on what He knows better, not on what you assume is right.

وَكَانَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ عَجُولًا - and the human being is extremely hasty. We want to rush to judgment, rush to conclusions, rush to get things very quickly. We lack patience in understanding the wisdom of timing. This impatience, this rush to judgment, is being cautioned against here. The human tendency is to want everything now, to demand immediate results, to pass judgment before all the facts are clear.

Ayah 12

وَجَعَلْنَا ٱلَّيْلَ وَٱلنَّهَارَ ءَايَتَيْنِ ۖ فَمَحَوْنَآ ءَايَةَ ٱلَّيْلِ وَجَعَلْنَآ ءَايَةَ ٱلنَّهَارِ مُبْصِرَةً لِّتَبْتَغُوا۟ فَضْلًا مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ وَلِتَعْلَمُوا۟ عَدَدَ ٱلسِّنِينَ وَٱلْحِسَابَ ۚ وَكُلَّ شَىْءٍ فَصَّلْنَـٰهُ تَفْصِيلًا

After discussing human hastiness, Allah ﷻ directs our attention to the natural world, where perfect timing exists: وَجَعَلْنَا ٱلَّيْلَ وَٱلنَّهَارَ ءَايَتَيْنِ - We have made the night and the day two miraculous signs. These aren’t merely physical phenomena; they’re آيَات - signs pointing to deeper truths.

For someone who truly reflects, the night and day themselves teach patience and proper timing. The night never rushes into the day, and the day never rushes into the night. Each takes its designated time. Everything comes at its proper moment. This is a direct response to the human tendency toward hastiness mentioned in the previous ayah.

فَمَحَوْنَآ ءَايَةَ ٱلَّيْلِ - then We erased the sign of the night. The word مَحَوْ means to erase something so thoroughly that no trace remains. When wind completely erases all signs of clouds, as though there were never any clouds there at all - that’s مَحَوْ. Allah ﷻ removed the night as though it was never there.

وَجَعَلْنَآ ءَايَةَ ٱلنَّهَارِ مُبْصِرَةً - and We made the sign of day as a means to see clearly. The day illuminates, allows vision, enables observation and action.

لِّتَبْتَغُوا۟ فَضْلًا مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ - so that you may pursue blessing from your Master. The daylight enables you to work, to earn your living, to seek out the provision that Allah ﷻ has made available. This isn’t automatic; you must pursue it, work for it during the hours of light.

وَلِتَعْلَمُوا۟ عَدَدَ ٱلسِّنِينَ وَٱلْحِسَابَ - and so that you may learn the count of years and calculation. The alternation of day and night provides the fundamental basis for measuring time. This is how we record history, track our lives, plan for the future. The calendar itself - the way we organize human civilization - rests on this divine arrangement.

وَكُلَّ شَىْءٍ فَصَّلْنَـٰهُ تَفْصِيلًا - and everything We have explained very thoroughly. This doesn’t refer only to the Quran. It refers to all of creation. The ingredients of human intellect needed to understand life, the faculties to put things in their proper place, the natural signs around us - everything you need to figure out life has already been given to you. All the tools have been provided. Even your ability to figure things out, to understand life, is actually Allah ﷻ explaining things to you. You didn’t figure it out independently; He enabled your understanding through the faculties and signs He provided.

Ayah 13

وَكُلَّ إِنسَـٰنٍ أَلْزَمْنَـٰهُ طَـٰٓئِرَهُۥ فِى عُنُقِهِۦ ۖ وَنُخْرِجُ لَهُۥ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ كِتَـٰبًا يَلْقَىٰهُ مَنشُورًا

This ayah introduces one of the Quran’s most powerful metaphors about personal accountability: وَكُلَّ إِنسَـٰنٍ أَلْزَمْنَـٰهُ طَـٰٓئِرَهُۥ فِى عُنُقِهِۦ - and every human being, We have fastened his bird to his neck.

The word أَلْزَمْنَا means to fasten something firmly, to commit something, to make something cling or stick to something else. طَـٰٓئِر literally means bird. So the literal translation would be: We’ve hung his bird around his neck. Obviously, this isn’t meant to be understood literally. The bird came to be used in Arabic for fate, for what’s going to happen in your life.

Why birds? Historically, the Arabs used birds for fortune-telling. They would release a bird and based on which direction it flew or where it landed, they would make decisions - should I marry her or not, should I travel or not. The bird’s flight determined their fate. You could say they were bird-brains about it. Additionally, in the desert, when you see birds hovering overhead, particularly vultures, what does that signal? Death. For people who believe in omens, it’s a bad sign. So طَـٰٓئِر came to mean both fate and the problems, the bad omens, the difficulties that a person will suffer in life.

Allah ﷻ is saying: whatever trials you will face in life, whatever difficulties await you, have already been fastened around your neck like a ticking clock. They didn’t come out of nowhere. It’s like a bell they put around a cow’s neck with a date on it marking when it will be slaughtered - you’re on schedule. Whatever problems you were going to face didn’t appear randomly. They were already decreed.

This is particularly relevant to the Israelites who had been through tremendous trials. You might wonder why they suffered so much. Allah ﷻ is saying: this isn’t unique to you. Every human being has their طَـٰٓئِر, their predetermined struggles, and they go through them on schedule.

But here’s the critical point: وَنُخْرِجُ لَهُۥ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ كِتَـٰبًا يَلْقَىٰهُ مَنشُورًا - and We will bring out for him on the Day of Resurrection a book that he will encounter spread open.

The word يَلْقَىٰ comes from لِقَاء, which means to meet or encounter someone. But he won’t be encountering a person - he’ll be encountering a book. “What’s this? I’m being introduced to this book?” And this book will be مَنشُورًا - spread out, unfurled. The root نَشَر means to spread out or publish. It’s a huge book where the pages keep spreading out, more and more and more. It’s an extensive record of his own deeds.

The message is profound: just because you had طَـٰٓئِر, just because you had what you might call “tough luck” in life, that doesn’t give you license to behave however you want. You can’t say, “Oh, I just had a tough life. That’s why I am the way I am.” No. Everybody has their predetermined problems and trials - that’s acknowledged. But your behavior is your behavior, and you will be held responsible for it. You’ll see your book. You won’t be able to justify your wrongdoing by saying, “Well, you know, I had a lot of problems in life. My family didn’t love me. I lost my job. I lived in a difficult neighborhood.” You can’t use those excuses. They won’t work.

Ayah 14

ٱقْرَأْ كِتَـٰبَكَ كَفَىٰ بِنَفْسِكَ ٱلْيَوْمَ عَلَيْكَ حَسِيبًا

The command is given directly: ٱقْرَأْ كِتَـٰبَكَ - read your book. Notice the shift from the previous ayah. There, يَلْقَىٰهُ - he encounters it - suggests meeting something unfamiliar, something he doesn’t recognize. “What is this?” But now the veil is lifted: “You don’t know what this is? This is your book. Read it.”

كَفَىٰ بِنَفْسِكَ ٱلْيَوْمَ عَلَيْكَ حَسِيبًا - you yourself are sufficient today as an auditor against yourself. You don’t need anyone else to take account of you. All you have to do is read. Your own self is enough to audit your own life.

This mirrors what happens in modern courtrooms when prosecutors want to establish the guilt of the accused. They’ll print out the defendant’s emails or messages and hand them over. “Could you read line three, please?” The accused hesitates: “You really want me to read this?” “Yes. Read it.” And they have to read their own incriminating words: “I will destroy you…” “Read your own book. You wrote it. This is yours.”

On the Day of Judgment, there will be no need for elaborate testimony, no complex proceedings. The book will be there, spread open, comprehensive and detailed. And the command will be simple and devastating: “Read.” Every deed recorded, every choice documented, every moment accounted for. And you yourself will be the most reliable auditor of your own actions, unable to deny what’s written in your own hand, in your own record.

This is the ultimate accountability - not imposed from outside, but recognized from within. The truth of your life, laid bare before you, undeniable and complete.

Ayah 15–16 · linked video: 04. Al-Isra’ (Ayah 15-16)

PRIMARY_AYAH: 15

Ayah 15

مَّنِ ٱهْتَدَىٰ فَإِنَّمَا يَهْتَدِى لِنَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَن ضَلَّ فَإِنَّمَا يَضِلُّ عَلَيْهَا ۚ وَلَا تَزِرُ وَازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرَىٰ ۗ وَمَا كُنَّا مُعَذِّبِينَ حَتَّىٰ نَبْعَثَ رَسُولًا

This ayah presents three fundamental principles of individual accountability before Allah ﷻ. Each principle serves as a cornerstone of Islamic justice and personal responsibility.

Personal Benefit and Personal Loss

The ayah begins: “Whoever holds onto guidance, he only does so for himself.” This echoes similar expressions found elsewhere in the Quran, such as “If you do good, you do good for yourselves.” The message is clear and unambiguous: when you choose to hold onto guidance, you are not benefiting anyone else. The rewards, the spiritual growth, the peace of mind—these belong entirely to you.

Conversely, “whoever gets lost, he is only getting lost against his own self, to his own harm, to his own disadvantage.” The word ضَلَّ carries the meaning of wandering away from the path, becoming lost. When someone strays from guidance, they harm no one but themselves. This is a powerful reminder that our spiritual choices have deeply personal consequences.

The Burden Each Soul Carries

The ayah continues with a profound metaphor: وَلَا تَزِرُ وَازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرَىٰ—“And no carrier of burdens will carry the burden of another.” To understand the depth of this statement, we must explore the word وِزْر.

The term wizr refers to a burden that you carry with you, like the belongings of a traveler. In the old days, travelers would wrap their possessions in a sheet, tie it to a stick, and carry it over their shoulder. This bundle—everything they owned—was called wizr. You could not abandon it; wherever you went, it went with you.

The verb form أَوْزَرَ means to conceal or hide, because the contents of the bundle are wrapped up inside the cloth. Allah ﷻ describes our deeds as something we carry around with us, something that remains with us even though it is hidden from view. The word also implies a hiding place in a mountain—a wazir—where people would store their valuables. They would find a corner or crevice, place their belongings inside, cover it with a rock, and hope to remember the location later.

This imagery teaches us that our deeds are both something we must carry and something we often try to hide. We walk around with our actions, our choices, our sins and good deeds, all bundled up and concealed—but never truly gone.

The ayah declares that no one will carry anyone else’s burden. You are responsible for your deeds, and others are responsible for theirs. This principle cuts through every excuse we might make. When children argue, “She hit me, so I hit her back three times,” or “I didn’t do my homework because my sister didn’t do hers,” the response is clear: وَلَا تَزِرُ وَازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرَىٰ. Nobody carries the burden of another. You are responsible for yourself; they are responsible for themselves.

This is a piece of Quranic wisdom you can apply in daily life. Why were you late? “Well, everyone else was late.” The ayah responds: each person carries their own burden.

The Necessity of a Messenger

The ayah concludes: وَمَا كُنَّا مُعَذِّبِينَ حَتَّىٰ نَبْعَثَ رَسُولًا—“And We would never punish until We send a messenger.” This establishes a fundamental principle of divine justice: Allah ﷻ does not destroy or punish any nation without first sending them clear guidance through a messenger.

Every civilization that faced destruction had received a messenger. This is how Allah ﷻ makes people truly accountable. The role of messengers is critical to understand: they did not come to carry people’s burdens. Rather, messengers came to ensure that people clearly understood their own burden.

The Jews believed that being the chosen people meant they were special and somehow exempt from full accountability. The Christians developed a theology where the Messiah carries the burden of sin. But Allah ﷻ clarifies: no, the entire point of sending messengers is so that you truly comprehend your responsibility. After receiving clear understanding through the messenger, if you still refuse to carry your burden, then accountability and consequences follow.

This principle of sending messengers before punishment reflects the perfect justice of Allah ﷻ. No one can claim ignorance or lack of warning when the consequences of their choices arrive.

Ayah 16

وَإِذَآ أَرَدْنَآ أَن نُّهْلِكَ قَرْيَةً أَمَرْنَا مُتْرَفِيهَا فَفَسَقُوا۟ فِيهَا فَحَقَّ عَلَيْهَا ٱلْقَوْلُ فَدَمَّرْنَـٰهَا تَدْمِيرًا

This ayah reveals Allah’s pattern in the destruction of civilizations. It describes a social and political process that leads societies toward their own collapse.

When Allah Intends to Destroy

The ayah begins: وَإِذَآ أَرَدْنَآ أَن نُّهْلِكَ قَرْيَةً—“And when We intend to destroy a town.” What follows is Allah’s process for the destruction of a civilization, a city, a nation. What does He do?

The Compelling of the Elite

أَمَرْنَا مُتْرَفِيهَا—this phrase contains profound meaning. The word أَمَرْنَا can be read two ways: with a shaddah (أَمَّرْنَا), it means “We gave authority to,” and without the shaddah (أَمَرْنَا), it means “We compelled.” Both readings reveal different dimensions of the same reality.

The word مُتْرَف comes from the root ت ر ف, which relates to being given excessive blessings to the point of deviation. A mutraf is someone who has enjoyed so many blessings, so much wealth and luxury, that they become delusional, obnoxious, and oblivious. They develop a sense of entitlement. They become the financial elite who feel inherently superior to others.

When wealth accumulates in their hands, something dangerous happens: they begin spending on vanities, on excess, on corrupt pursuits. Even more concerning, they become the standard for everyone else in society. When the wealthy elite appear in movies, give tours of their mansions on television, or become embroiled in various scandals, the average person watches and thinks, “I wish I could do those things. I want to be like them.”

The entertainment elite, the financial elite—these مُتْرَفِين become the model for corruption throughout the entire society. Allah ﷻ says He compels them in this direction, and the result is inevitable.

Authority in the Hands of the Wealthy

The second reading—“We give authority to the financial elite”—reveals an even more powerful principle. Consider what happens when those whose primary concern is wealth accumulation gain positions of governmental power.

In any healthy government, positions of authority—whether the presidency, governorship, or ministerial roles—are meant to be public service. Public servants have their first obligation to whom? The public. These positions are meant to be selfless, not avenues for personal enrichment.

Consider the example from the time of Umar (RA). He would audit governors before they took office, documenting their assets. After their term ended, he would audit them again. Any wealth accumulated during their term of service would be returned to the people, because public service means exactly that—serving the public, not enriching yourself. This removed the incentive for those seeking personal gain and ensured that only those genuinely committed to serving people would take on such roles.

Compare this to governments today. Why do people pursue government positions? Often for the money, for themselves. Government jobs frequently offer the best benefits, the most security, the most comfortable arrangements. The result? Inefficiency, laziness, and a culture where bureaucrats create obstacles rather than provide service. Visit any government office and observe how reluctant employees are to help, how they generate endless paperwork and demand repeated appointments.

When Wealth Meets Power

Now imagine the scenario this ayah describes: the wealthiest—millionaires and billionaires—become the ones holding governmental power. What will they do with that power? They will use it to expand their already existing financial empires. They will sign contracts that benefit their interests. They will work with lobby groups. They will position themselves so that after leaving public office, they can earn enormous sums as consultants, leveraging their insider knowledge and connections.

This entire mechanism becomes the elite in power serving whom? The elite serving the elite. When this happens, corruption spreads throughout the land—فَفَسَقُوا۟ فِيهَا—“and they created corruption in it.”

This principle applies universally. It does not matter whether the government is a kingdom or a democracy. It does not matter which country. These are timeless social, ethical, and political realities. When the financial elite becomes the politically powerful—and they often gain power precisely because of their wealth—the system itself reveals its corruption. The candidate with the most money gets their message out the most. This alone tells us something is deeply wrong.

The Cascade of Corruption

When the elite control power, every institution bends to serve their interests. Laws, regulations, even agencies meant to protect people—like food and drug administrations—end up serving corporations and the already wealthy. Who gets harmed? The masses of people.

Another insidious aspect emerges: these powerful people give jobs to whom? Their own connections. The poor young man who worked hard through university, who earned his degree with dedication, cannot find employment. Meanwhile, the nephew of a governor—someone everyone knows barely attended class, who bought his degree—walks into a job without even interviewing.

Imagine the rage this builds in people. The qualified graduate watches someone utterly unqualified receive opportunities simply because of family connections. This creates a boiling resentment. Crime rises. A rigid class society develops where the haves and have-nots begin to hate each other.

These are all elements of a society heading toward destruction. When Allah ﷻ decides to destroy a nation, He allows the financial elite to take charge. What follows is a domino effect—everything else falls into place automatically.

Cultural Authority and Social Enslavement

Authority is not limited to political power. There is also social and cultural authority. Who holds cultural authority for our children and youth today? For many, it is entertainment channels, the sports industry, the movie industry. The way people dress is dictated by cultural authorities, and deviating from these norms means defying an invisible power structure.

We speak of freedom in the West, particularly in the United States—freedom of ideas, freedom of expression, freedom to be whoever you want. Yet observe high school students. Are they truly free? They are forced to dress certain ways, to belong to specific subcultures: the jocks, the hip-hop kids, the heavy metal fans, the preppies. You must fit into a clan; you cannot simply be yourself.

One young person, dressed in the uniform of his crowd—baseball cap sideways, pants hanging low, oversized T-shirt—approached in tears. “Why are you crying?” He responded, “I don’t want to dress like this. But if I don’t, I’ll get beat up. I don’t want to walk like I’m physically impaired, but I have to, or people will make fun of me.”

This is cultural enslavement. It exists not only among youth but among adults as well. Where will you hold your daughter’s wedding? “They held theirs at the country club.” You want to do it at the masjid? “What will people say? You can’t embarrass the family like that.”

These pressures create financial difficulties for families. When someone cannot match what the elite did, others judge their children as less worthy of marriage proposals, less valuable as human beings. This creates hatred between classes. When the poor try to imitate the wealthy, they become poorer, putting themselves into debt. The gap widens.

Consider someone earning minimum wage who cannot pay rent but installs expensive spinning rims on his old car. Why? Because he saw a wealthy person do it in a music video, and now he wants to feel like he is worth something. He gets paid on Friday, and by Saturday, he is borrowing money. This is mental enslavement—feeling compelled to act this way, genuinely believing that without these markers of wealth, he is worthless.

This phenomenon is not limited to one culture or ethnicity. Whether in Latin American neighborhoods, South Asian communities, or anywhere else in the world, people put themselves into difficulty just to imitate the elite. This is أَمَرْنَا مُتْرَفِيهَا in action—the elite setting standards that destroy the fabric of society.

The Inevitable Conclusion

فَحَقَّ عَلَيْهَا ٱلْقَوْلُ—“Then the guarantee of punishment materializes against them.” All the consequences of this corruption become reality. The word حَقَّ means it becomes binding, inevitable, guaranteed.

فَدَمَّرْنَـٰهَا تَدْمِيرًا—“Then We crush that society completely, We completely annihilate that society.” The repetition of the root word—dammarnahā tadmiran—emphasizes total destruction.

SubhanAllah, Allah ﷻ describes the social and political ingredients for the destruction of a civilization. This is also what the Israelites experienced. Their social elite became their political elite, and when these two forces merged, corruption became inevitable and comprehensive.

This ayah serves as a warning for all times and all places: when wealth concentrates in the hands of those who hold power, and when these elites set the cultural and social standards for everyone else, a society has set itself on the path toward its own destruction.

Ayah 17–23 · linked video: 05. Al-Isra’ (Ayah 17-23)

Surah Al-Isra: Ayahs 17-23

Ayah 17

وَكَمْ أَهْلَكْنَا مِنَ ٱلْقُرُونِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ نُوحٍ ۗ وَكَفَىٰ بِرَبِّكَ بِذُنُوبِ عِبَادِهِۦ خَبِيرًۢا بَصِيرًا

“How many generations have We destroyed after Nuh? And sufficient is your Lord, concerning the sins of His servants, as Acquainted and Seeing.”

The previous ayah established the process of destruction that befalls nations when they exceed their bounds. Now Allah ﷻ asks a rhetorical question: How many towns have We already destroyed? This isn’t seeking information but rather emphasizing a historical pattern that stretches back beyond Nuh (AS).

The ayah concludes with two of Allah’s attributes that reveal how He ﷻ witnesses the destruction of nations: خَبِيرًۢا and بَصِيرًا. Understanding the relationship between these two names deepens our appreciation of Allah’s knowledge.

Khabir refers to someone who has news or knowledge of something, while Basir indicates someone who directly sees it. There’s a fundamental difference between hearing about an event and witnessing it firsthand. The Arabs have a famous saying: “The news is not like the eyewitness account.” When you hear about an accident, you have information, but you didn’t witness it yourself.

Allah ﷻ first mentions that He has complete knowledge of what His slaves did, but then takes it further by describing Himself as Basir—His knowledge isn’t secondhand information but rather a direct witness account. There’s another dimension to consider: what happened in the past, you know about, but what’s happening now, you see. The word Khabir captures Allah’s knowledge of the destructions of nations from the past—He knew all along the sins of those who came before. But Basir emphasizes that He sees what’s going on right now. He saw those past nations in real-time as they sinned, and He continues to witness the present in the same way. Allah ﷻ remains an active witness to the sins of His servants, both then and now.

Ayah 18

مَّن كَانَ يُرِيدُ ٱلْعَاجِلَةَ عَجَّلْنَا لَهُۥ فِيهَا مَا نَشَآءُ لِمَن نُّرِيدُ ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَا لَهُۥ جَهَنَّمَ يَصْلَىٰهَا مَذْمُومًا مَّدْحُورًا

“Whoever should desire the immediate—We hasten for him from it what We will to whom We intend. Then We have made for him Hellfire, which he will enter to burn, condemned and banished.”

This ayah presents a powerful principle about how Allah ﷻ decides what kind of life a person will live and what opportunities they’ll encounter. The term العاجلة means “the rushed one” or the immediate. Using this particular grammatical form allows it to serve as an adjective for other things not explicitly mentioned—worldly compensations, material success and so forth.

Allah ﷻ says that whoever wanted this quick, worldly life, “We rush it for him.” But notice the qualifications that follow: “whatever We will” and “for whoever We want.” This reveals something profound: not everyone who aspires to worldly success achieves it. Everyone who wants to be a millionaire doesn’t become one. Allah ﷻ expedites the process for some, opening doors for them, while for others He doesn’t. The distribution of worldly success isn’t purely based on effort or desire—it’s ultimately in Allah’s hands.

Then comes the severe consequence: “Then We install for him Hellfire.” The word يَصْلَىٰهَا indicates he will be cast into it, and the passive-sounding construction emphasizes how easily it happens. The person will be مَذْمُومًا —condemned, worthy of condemnation for something despicable. And مَّدْحُورًا —kicked out, expelled. Picture someone not just entering prison but being kicked into it. This is the one who pursued only the immediate pleasures, gaining some of them in this world but losing everything in the next.

Ayah 19

وَمَنْ أَرَادَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ وَسَعَىٰ لَهَا سَعْيَهَا وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ كَانَ سَعْيُهُم مَّشْكُورًا

“But whoever desires the Hereafter and exerts the effort due to it while he is a believer—it is those whose effort is ever appreciated.”

In contrast to those who seek only the immediate, this ayah describes the one who intended the afterlife, who made up their mind that their goals would be for Jannah. The word سَعَىٰ means to rush or run with effort—this is why the walking between Safa and Marwa is called sa’i, the running.

But notice the eloquent addition: سَعْيَهَا —“its effort” or “the effort worthy of it.” Grammatically, when you add a pronoun referring back to something mentioned, it creates emphasis. The ayah doesn’t just say “he made an effort toward it,” but rather “he made an effort that truly is the effort worthy of the Akhirah.” He didn’t just put in some effort—he put in effort recognizing what the afterlife is truly worth. He valued that effort as he valued the destination itself.

What Allah ﷻ describes here is someone whose efforts correspond to their conviction. If you’re truly convinced you’re getting immense rewards in Jannah, your efforts will reflect that conviction. Your laziness will disappear accordingly. This isn’t about merely going through the motions—it’s about striving with a heart that genuinely believes in what it’s working toward.

The ayah adds another condition: وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ —“and he remained a believer.” He held onto his Iman throughout his striving. Then comes the beautiful conclusion: فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ كَانَ سَعْيُهُم مَّشْكُورًا —“those people, their efforts have always been appreciated.” The word مَشْكُورًا means thanked, acknowledged, appreciated. Allah ﷻ Himself acknowledges the efforts of those who make these kinds of strides for the Akhirah. May Allah ﷻ make us all from among them.

Ayah 20

كُلًّا نُّمِدُّ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ وَهَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ مِنْ عَطَآءِ رَبِّكَ ۚ وَمَا كَانَ عَطَآءُ رَبِّكَ مَحْظُورًا

“To each We extend—to these and to those—from the gift of your Lord. And never has the gift of your Lord been restricted.”

The ayah addresses both groups mentioned previously: those who work toward the Dunya and those who work toward the Akhirah. كُلًّا —every one of these groups—نُّمِدُّ —We are extending. Allah ﷻ has allowed both to continue, letting the competition unfold. He has allowed the timer to run and extended their time in this world.

The phrase مِنْ عَطَآءِ رَبِّكَ carries multiple meanings. First, it means the time we have to earn the Akhirah is itself a grant from Allah ﷻ. But it also means Allah ﷻ has extended His means to both groups. The believer will find a job; the disbeliever will find a job. The believer will have a house; the disbeliever will have a house. Both have been given things in this world—all from the granting of your Master.

Then comes a critical principle: وَمَا كَانَ عَطَآءُ رَبِّكَ مَحْظُورًا —“the grants of your Master have never, ever been, and will never be, restricted.” The word مَحْظُورًا comes from the root meaning to build a fence, to protect something by walling it off. A person might want to build high walls around their house for privacy, to create a protected courtyard where the family can be free without anyone watching from outside. This reflects traditional Islamic architecture, where courtyards were placed in the middle of homes rather than as front porches or backyards, allowing families privacy under the open sky.

But Allah’s favors haven’t been fenced off or denied to anyone. Don’t think that only good things should come to believers and that disbelievers shouldn’t enjoy any blessings. The grants of your Master are not restricted based on faith alone in this world. Both groups receive from His generosity, though their ultimate destinations differ vastly.

Ayah 21

ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ فَضَّلْنَا بَعْضَهُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ ۚ وَلَلْـَٔاخِرَةُ أَكْبَرُ دَرَجَـٰتٍ وَأَكْبَرُ تَفْضِيلًا

“Look how We have favored some of them over others. But the Hereafter is greater in degrees and greater in distinction.”

Allah ﷻ commands us to contemplate: Look at how We’ve given preference to some over others in this world. People exist at different economic levels, with different opportunities, educational capacities and competencies. Allah ﷻ has given some more than others in various worldly aspects.

Then comes the statement about the afterlife: وَلَلْـَٔاخِرَةُ أَكْبَرُ دَرَجَـٰتٍ —“the Akhirah is greater in darajat.” This phrase contains a beautiful eloquent play on words. According to normal grammatical principles, when you want to express “greater in” something, you use the singular form. You would say “greater in rank” (daraja), not “greater in ranks” (darajat). But the Quran uses the plural form here deliberately.

When you say “the Akhirah is greater in rank,” you’re implying one ranking system where things are numbered: first, second, third, fourth, fifth. But by saying “greater in ranks” (plural), Allah ﷻ indicates the Akhirah is even more vast in terms of multitudes of ranks. There isn’t just one thing being graded—there are multiple criteria, multiple skill sets being evaluated.

Think of it like sports statistics: there’s free throw average, steals, defense and various other categories. In some areas you might rank at the top, in others at the bottom and in some areas somewhere in the middle. Similarly, we’re going to be evaluated on multiple things. It’s not just one criterion. That’s why we’re supposed to meet a bare minimum in all our obligations, but if Allah ﷻ has given you a gift in something specific, you shouldn’t pretend to be humble about it—you should exhaust that gift for good.

Perhaps your patience isn’t the greatest, or your dealings with people could use improvement. You’re not a master in every area, but you meet minimal standards. Maybe you’re rough around the edges in social interactions—customer service might not be your calling. But you might be an excellent writer, genuinely gifted with words and creativity. Don’t tell yourself, “I’m not even a nice person to others, so how can I write?” That’s wrong thinking, because your intentions aren’t for people—they’re recognizing what gift Allah ﷻ has given you and exhausting it, giving your best.

You should acknowledge your shortcomings while simultaneously maximizing your strengths. The good you do shouldn’t erase awareness of your flaws in your own mind—those are other darajat, other categories. But you should try to do more and more good in whatever gift Allah ﷻ has given you, hoping that in His sight it will compensate for some of your other flaws. The real problem happens when you take your good qualities and think they should cover your flaws in this world, expecting people to excuse your shortcomings because of your strengths. That’s problematic.

If, for instance, you’re lazy but a good teacher, your teaching doesn’t give you license to remain lazy, angry or problematic in other ways. It doesn’t cover those flaws. But you’re hoping that with Allah ﷻ—who knows all your shortcomings as an open book—the extra effort you’re making, the exhausting work you’re putting into your gift, will be appreciated. كَانَ سَعْيُهُم مَّشْكُورًا —it will be appreciated.

The ayah also says وَأَكْبَرُ تَفْضِيلًا —“greater in distinction.” The Akhirah isn’t just about multiple ranks, but also about how preference and distinction are distributed, who receives favor and who doesn’t in far more significant ways than this temporary world.

Ayah 22

لَّا تَجْعَلْ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَـٰهًا ءَاخَرَ فَتَقْعُدَ مَذْمُومًا مَّخْذُولًا

“Do not make another deity with Allah, or you will sit condemned and forsaken.”

After describing the realities of this life and the next, Allah ﷻ issues a stark command: Don’t associate, don’t place alongside Allah ﷻ any other entity worthy of worship or obedience.

The consequence is expressed with the word فَتَقْعُدَ —“then you will remain sitting.” This implies someone who was standing and sat down, or someone who stands around with nothing to do. It describes sitting around without getting any work done, just remaining idle. When someone asks, “What are you doing?” and the answer is “Just chillin’ on the couch”—that’s qu’ud, just sitting there. This is different from temporary rest while working—this is being permanently benched, sidelined from life itself.

The one who commits shirk will miss out on life entirely. They’ll be on the sidelines, benched from what really matters. And what will they end up being? مَذْمُومًا —condemned. And مَّخْذُولًا —forsaken, deceived by what they thought was a friend.

The word خَذَلَ is used in the Quran for Shaytan. It describes someone who pretends to be your friend, gives you advice, but at the hour of need disappears. You discover their true nature when it’s too late. That’s the useless kind of friend, and madh-dhul is the victim of that betrayal. The one who does shirk will find their entire life passing by without urgency, no sense that anything is wrong, just sitting back—until suddenly they find themselves in a position where they’re condemned and utterly forsaken.

Ayah 23

۞ وَقَضَىٰ رَبُّكَ أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوٓا۟ إِلَّآ إِيَّاهُ وَبِٱلْوَٰلِدَيْنِ إِحْسَـٰنًا ۚ إِمَّا يَبْلُغَنَّ عِندَكَ ٱلْكِبَرَ أَحَدُهُمَآ أَوْ كِلَاهُمَا فَلَا تَقُل لَّهُمَآ أُفٍّ وَلَا تَنْهَرْهُمَا وَقُل لَّهُمَا قَوْلًا كَرِيمًا

“And your Lord has decreed that you not worship except Him, and to parents, good treatment. Whether one or both of them reach old age with you, say not to them ‘uff’ and do not repel them but speak to them a noble word.”

Scholars note that this passage represents the Quranic equivalent of what was revealed to Musa (AS) in the Torah—the fundamental commandments that form the foundation of moral society. This is the “Thou Shall” passage of the Quran, establishing essential principles that prevent corruption and destruction.

These commandments appear right after Allah ﷻ mentioned destroying nations and the warnings about causing corruption in the earth. Why? Because if you lose these commandments, you will end up causing corruption. When you don’t respect these principles, society will be destroyed. These are the foundational rules for a healthy society. We saw earlier the social laws regarding community interactions. Now we encounter the moral principles, the ethics that must govern a Muslim society. All morality starts at the individual level, then spreads to the family.

The ayah begins: قَضَىٰ رَبُّكَ —“Your Lord has decreed.” This isn’t a suggestion or recommendation; it’s a firm declaration. أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوٓا۟ إِلَّآ إِيَّاهُ —“that you will not be enslaved to anyone except He exclusively.” The phrasing emphasizes exclusive devotion to Allah ﷻ alone.

Immediately, without pause, comes: وَبِٱلْوَٰلِدَيْنِ إِحْسَـٰنًا —“and to both parents, excellence.” Notice the grammatical structure: the verb isn’t even mentioned, which highlights the urgency of the matter. It’s not “be good to parents” in a complete sentence, but rather “Parents—excellence!” The urgency is palpable, like saying “No trouble!”—it’s a warning and command compressed into the shortest possible form.

Allah ﷻ went from Himself—the fundamental relationship between slave and Master—directly to parents. He didn’t mention prophets or angels or any other relationships. He went straight from Master to parents. This placement is profound.

The term الْوَٰلِدَيْنِ specifically means your biological parents—the ones who physically gave birth to you. This is different from terms that could mean adoptive parents or guardians. Whether they’re nice people or not doesn’t matter. Whether you have a good relationship with them, whether they understand you, whether they hate your Islam, whether you get along with them, whether they were kind during your upbringing—none of that is a condition here. Just as your relationship with Allah ﷻ is unconditional, your next unconditional relationship is with your biological parents.

The command is إِحْسَـٰنًا —excellence, the highest level of beautiful conduct. Consider this carefully: What’s the starting point of Islam with Allah ﷻ? Worship, servitude. What’s the highest point you can reach with Allah ﷻ? Ihsan, excellence in worship as if you see Him. But the starting point with parents is ihsan. The ayah isn’t talking about the highest expectations—it’s talking about the minimum expectations. You will be the best you can possibly be with both parents.

This is the first consequence, the first practical manifestation: if you’ve truly accepted Allah ﷻ as Master, you will act like His slave before your parents. Your ego, your pride, your talking back, your sarcasm, your rolling of the eyes—all of that will disappear because you must have the best possible behavior before your parents.

Then comes the specific situation: إِمَّا يَبْلُغَنَّ عِندَكَ ٱلْكِبَرَ أَحَدُهُمَآ أَوْ كِلَاهُمَا —“Whether one or both of them reach old age with you.” This highlights the most challenging time, when parents become elderly and dependent, perhaps difficult to deal with.

فَلَا تَقُل لَّهُمَآ أُفٍّ —“Do not say to them ‘uff.’” This is the smallest expression of annoyance, the slightest sound of frustration. If this tiny expression is forbidden, then everything beyond it—harsh words, raising your voice, showing anger—is obviously prohibited with even greater emphasis.

وَلَا تَنْهَرْهُمَا —“And do not repel them.” Don’t push them away, don’t brush them off, don’t make them feel unwanted or burdensome.

وَقُل لَّهُمَا قَوْلًا كَرِيمًا —“But speak to them a noble word.” Not just polite speech, but kariman—noble, generous, honoring speech that elevates them and shows them dignity.

This is probably one of the hardest commandments in Islam, particularly in contemporary times. Perhaps it wasn’t as difficult in societies built on respect, where the concept of honoring elders was woven into the culture. But modern society has injected individualism into people like a drug. The primary concern has become the self. Many have no concept of what it means to respect an elder, let alone a parent. They were never raised on that foundation.

The shock of this cultural shift becomes apparent in everyday interactions. When young people casually refer to their father as “my old man” or call him by his first name, speaking about him as if he’s an employee they’re about to fire—this reveals how deeply the culture of parental respect has eroded. In traditional cultures, such casual disrespect would be unthinkable.

Why are these two things—worship of Allah ﷻ and excellence toward parents—so intimately connected? The relationship works both ways. Allah ﷻ takes care of us, provides for us, feeds us, shows us mercy, gives us love. But before we recognize that Allah ﷻ does all of this from the unseen, who does all those things in the visible realm? Our parents. The first introduction a child has to love, care, provision and mercy is through their mother and father.

In the beginning, a baby’s entire world is the mother. Even when the father tries to hold the baby, the infant might refuse, preferring how the mother feels. As the child grows older, the father becomes the one who gives breaks from chores, who takes them out for ice cream with a conspiratorial “Don’t tell Mom.” There’s a relationship, a bond. The child’s entire world revolves around these two people. When you’re out of the house, what do young children constantly ask? “Where’s Mama? Where’s Baba?” That’s their world.

If you can’t learn to appreciate your parents who are present and visible, then logically you’ll have a hard time—a really hard time—appreciating Allah ﷻ who is beyond the seen. There’s a direct correlation. Our inability to show gratitude and appreciation to the most direct favor of Allah ﷻ—our parents—has everything to do with our inability to pray on time, to make dua sincerely, to cry before Allah ﷻ. These failures are directly related. The heart that cannot humble itself before parents whom it can see will struggle to humble itself before the Lord it cannot see.

Ayah 23–25 · linked video: 06. Al-Isra’ (Ayah 23-25)

Surah Al-Isra: Ayahs 23-25

Ayah 23

۞ وَقَضَىٰ رَبُّكَ أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوٓا۟ إِلَّآ إِيَّاهُ وَبِٱلْوَٰلِدَيْنِ إِحْسَـٰنًا ۚ إِمَّا يَبْلُغَنَّ عِندَكَ ٱلْكِبَرَ أَحَدُهُمَآ أَوْ كِلَاهُمَا فَلَا تَقُل لَّهُمَآ أُفٍّ وَلَا تَنْهَرْهُمَا وَقُل لَّهُمَا قَوْلًا كَرِيمًا

And your Lord has decreed that you worship none but Him, and to parents, good treatment. Whether one or both of them reach old age while with you, say not to them a word of disrespect, nor repel them but speak to them a noble word.

This ayah is not simply about the general rights of parents. It specifically addresses the rights of parents when they become difficult to deal with, when they reach old age. The critical phrase here is إِمَّا يَبْلُغَنَّ عِندَكَ ٱلْكِبَرَ (whether they reach old age while with you). Notice the emphasis on عِندَكَ (with you) - in your midst, in your company, alongside you.

When parents grow old in a different state or country, the situation becomes easier to manage. Why? Because when a conversation becomes challenging, there’s an escape route. The connection drops, someone’s at the door, there’s always an excuse. But when they live with you or next door, when they see your car parked and know you’re home, when they’re knocking on your door asking why you didn’t visit yesterday or commenting on how you keep your house or raise your children - there’s no escape from these moments that test your patience.

As parents age, they develop an uncanny ability to press exactly the right buttons. They know precisely what will get under your skin, and sometimes they do it without even thinking. The dynamics differ between mothers and fathers. When a mother is in a bad mood, the situation can become particularly intense - even fathers might retreat and go for a walk rather than engage. The worst scenario is when both parents team up: “Your mother’s right. Listen to your mother. Whatever she said, I didn’t hear it, but you listen.”

Modern life compounds these challenges. We’ve become increasingly independent at earlier ages, a byproduct of our culture of individualism. We want our own rooms, our own friends, our own spaces. We create boundaries: “This is my room, these are my toys, this is my computer, this is my iPod - don’t touch it.” The first people children begin to shut out are their parents. Friends start replacing parents in importance.

The more we stop talking to our parents, the more we convince ourselves they don’t understand us. But whose fault is this distance? As children grow older, we make assumptions: “They just won’t understand.” Meanwhile, parents perceive this silence as disrespect. “He never talks to me. He doesn’t think I’m worth anything. He thinks he understands everything and I know nothing.”

This frustration builds in their hearts. They remember: “I used to change your diapers. I carried you on my shoulder, lost sleep all night taking you to doctors. You used to kick me in your sleep and I still patted you down to sleep. Now you’re going to treat me this way?” All that accumulated frustration erupts when you finally visit after a month. You think you’re being nice by visiting, but instead you’re met with an explosion of built-up emotions. You slam the door and walk out, thinking this is impossible.

This is modern reality for many of us. We must fix our relationships with our parents - this is a priority for all of us, especially those who are married. It takes conscious effort to make time, to call, to visit, to step away from other obligations. You must navigate family politics: “You’re going to your mother? When will you be back? What did she say about me?” You have to fight through all of this and still give time to your parents.

Will you hear agitating things from them? Yes. Allah ﷻ mentioned in Surah Nahl about old age, describing how after knowing so much, a person reaches a point where he doesn’t know anything. Your father hands you a computer: “I keep pressing it, nothing’s working.” You’re thinking, “Why don’t you know this?” But you must swallow your frustration and say, “Okay, let me try to help.” Then he says, “You don’t know anything.” At that point, what do you do? You respond with humility: “I don’t know anything, Dad. Let me just try, okay?” If he says you’ll make it worse, you reply, “I’m so sorry if I messed it up. Let’s fix it together.”

It takes enormous patience to maintain these conversations rather than snapping back: “Forget it. Who needs this? It’s so simple - just click send. Why is this so hard for you?” You cannot be condescending. This test comes to all of us, regardless of how much knowledge we possess.
