It doesn’t matter if you’re a teacher, a student, a scholar - all of us face this trial.

The word أُفٍّ originally referred to old dirt or filth lying around, something disgusting. It evolved into an expression of frustration, disgust and dislike. Some people don’t even say a word - they just take a deep exhale. For others, it’s a facial expression. Whatever form it takes, فَلَا تَقُل لَّهُمَآ أُفٍّ (do not say to them “uff”) - don’t express disgust, frustration, anger or dislike.

وَلَا تَنْهَرْهُمَا (nor repel them) - the verb نَهَرَ comes from roots meaning to splash hard, like splashing water forcefully on your face. Scolding is described this way because to the one receiving it, it feels like being slapped or splashed. Those yelling at the top of their lungs are literally splashing as well - spitting as they shout. Don’t scold them. Don’t raise your voice at them. Don’t talk back. Don’t snap. Don’t lose control around them.

Instead, وَقُل لَّهُمَا قَوْلًا كَرِيمًا (speak to them a noble word) - speak to both of them in graceful, noble terms, showing them ultimate respect.

Ayah 24

وَٱخْفِضْ لَهُمَا جَنَاحَ ٱلذُّلِّ مِنَ ٱلرَّحْمَةِ وَقُل رَّبِّ ٱرْحَمْهُمَا كَمَا رَبَّيَانِى صَغِيرًا

And lower to them the wing of humility out of mercy and say, “My Lord, have mercy upon them as they brought me up when I was small.”

We’re still not finished with the discussion of parents. وَٱخْفِضْ لَهُمَا جَنَاحَ ٱلذُّلِّ (lower for them the wing of humility) - this is divine speech at its most profound. A bird has the ability to fly. It raises its wings to take flight, but when it lowers its wings, it’s saying, “I’m going to land.” Even though it possesses the power of flight, it chooses not to use it.

This isn’t just about humility - it’s about humility despite having the power to not be humble. There’s a significant difference. Anyone can be humble when they have no power. When you get pulled over by a police officer, you become very humble: “I’m so sorry, officer. I didn’t realize I was going 75. Can’t believe I did that in a school zone.” You’ll be humbled before you’re hauled off to jail, but you’re in a position where you have no power.

With your parents, especially as you grow older, the situation differs. Perhaps you’ve become the breadwinner in the household. Perhaps they’ve retired. Perhaps you’re in a position of authority. Perhaps they come to you for advice. Perhaps you’re taking care of all the bills, the paperwork, the taxes, the healthcare. You’re in a position to say, “Hey, why are you talking to me like this? I’m the one taking care of everything.”

Allah ﷻ says: lower your wings. I know you have wings. Just pretend you don’t have them when dealing with them. Be what you were when you were eight years old. Just be that child again.

مِنَ ٱلرَّحْمَةِ (out of mercy) - this phrase has three layers of meaning. First, show them mercy out of compassion for them as they’re reaching old age. Second, show them mercy because of the mercy they showed you. You were far more obnoxious and far more agitating than they will ever be. They showed you far more love than you will ever show them.

You will face all kinds of problems in life, but someone who will never cease to love you is your mother. The overwhelming majority of mothers never stop loving their children. You can lose the love of your father - he can say “Get out of the house.” But what will your mother say? “No, no, no, my baby. Let him stay. He won’t do it again.”

Fathers aren’t naturally softies. You have to work on that relationship. It doesn’t come naturally to fathers because men aren’t as emotionally open or connected as women are. The Messenger of Allah ﷺ put in the work - he was an extremely sensitive man. But emotional intelligence, while natural for mothers, requires work for fathers. When a child comes to the father saying, “You know what happened at school?” the father might respond, “I don’t know, talk to me later,” not realizing how much that hurts. The mother, seeing this, asks, “Tell me, what happened?” Even if the child says, “I don’t know, I forgot,” he still feels good because his mother heard him.

Your mother will never stop loving you. As annoying as she might get, as angry as she might become, the love she has for you - you will never find anybody in your life who loves you like that. Ever. I don’t care if you’re 90 years old and your mother is still alive. Your mother still loves you as she did when you were born.

The third meaning of مِنَ ٱلرَّحْمَةِ is this: if you want a lot of mercy from Allah ﷻ, you better be humble to your parents.

The word خَفَضَ الْجَنَاحَ (lowering the wing) has another meaning - when a bird protects its nest. When a snake tries to eat the eggs, the bird spreads its wings over the nest so the snake bites the bird itself. The bird is willing to die, be poisoned or be injured so that its innocent eggs underneath, which don’t even know what’s happening, remain safe. This image of a parent protecting a child is reversed in the Quran to describe a child protecting a parent.

This reversal is captured at the end of the ayah: رَّبِّ ٱرْحَمْهُمَا كَمَا رَبَّيَانِى صَغِيرًا (My Lord, have mercy upon them as they brought me up when I was small). The word رَبَّيَا (they brought me up) comes from رَبَّى يُرَبِّي تَرْبِيَة - not the same word as رَبّ (Lord). This root means to ensure growth, to take care of one’s development. “Show mercy to both of them the way they ensured my growth when I was small, the way they took care of me as I was growing up.”

Ayah 25

رَّبُّكُمْ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا فِى نُفُوسِكُمْ ۚ إِن تَكُونُوا۟ صَـٰلِحِينَ فَإِنَّهُۥ كَانَ لِلْأَوَّٰبِينَ غَفُورًا

Your Lord is most knowing of what is within yourselves. If you should be righteous - then indeed He is ever, to the often returning, Forgiving.

When someone hears these ayahs, they sometimes respond: “Brother, I know what you’re talking about, but you don’t know my parents. I know the Quran says that, but you don’t know my mother. She’s so mean. I don’t know what to do.”

Allah ﷻ addresses this concern in the very next ayah: رَّبُّكُمْ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا فِى نُفُوسِكُمْ (Your Lord knows better what’s in your heart, what you have inside of you). He knows your situation. Don’t worry about that.

إِن تَكُونُوا۟ صَـٰلِحِينَ (If you are righteous) - if you’re the innocent party in this situation - فَإِنَّهُۥ كَانَ لِلْأَوَّٰبِينَ غَفُورًا (then He has always been, for those who return, extremely forgiving).

The word أَوَّٰبِين is particularly powerful. أَوَّاب comes from أَوَبَ يَؤُوبُ أَوْبًا, which means to come back from a long journey. Imagine you’ve traveled 800 miles in the wrong direction, and you realize you should head back. Are you as motivated to turn around and make that long journey home? Or are you exhausted, thinking, “I don’t know. Maybe I should just move here now. I’ve got all my stuff here. I came all this far. Might as well stay. It’s too much work to go back.”

An أَوَّاب is someone who went far down the road of sin - they traveled really far. When they look back and realize they’ve traveled thousands of miles in sin, they start thinking, “What’s the point of even trying to go back? I’m not going to make it all the way back.”

Allah ﷻ says: for those who will try to make that journey, that long journey back, He will be especially forgiving. He wants those people to take that long journey. إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ لِلْأَوَّٰبِينَ غَفُورًا (Indeed He is ever, to the often returning, Forgiving).

Notice this is mentioned particularly in the context of relationships with parents. You might be thinking, “It’s been years. I’ve been terrible to my parents. We’ve gone way too far down this road. The damage is beyond repair. Nothing can be done about it.”

Allah ﷻ responds: I know you’ve traveled far. Just head back, and let Me give you an incentive - I’ll forgive all of that past. Your motivation shouldn’t just be that relations will get better. Your motivation is that Allah ﷻ will forgive everything that happened before.

This promise of divine forgiveness is immensely powerful.

Ayah 26–31 · linked video: 07. Al-Isra’ (Ayah 26-31)

Surah Al-Isra: Ayahs 26-31

Ayah 26

وَءَاتِ ذَا ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ حَقَّهُۥ وَٱلْمِسْكِينَ وَٱبْنَ ٱلسَّبِيلِ وَلَا تُبَذِّرْ تَبْذِيرًا

After addressing our obligations toward parents, Allah ﷻ now extends the circle of responsibility to the rest of the family. The wisdom in this sequence is profound - when you can fulfill the rights of your parents properly, dealing with extended family becomes manageable. While you may not see them as frequently or feel the same level of obligation, the duty toward them remains real and significant.

The ayah says وَءَاتِ ذَا ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ حَقَّهُۥ - “Give the one who possesses closeness to you his right.” Notice the singular form ذَا (the one). Allah ﷻ didn’t use a plural construction that would lump everyone together. This grammatical choice teaches us that every single person deserves individual attention - every sibling, grandparent, child, uncle, nephew. You should think about them individually, not just as a collective group.

The ayah then expands beyond family: وَٱلْمِسْكِينَ - the one in financial need, the miskeen. Then وَٱبْنَ ٱلسَّبِيلِ - literally “the son of the road,” referring to the traveler who is not from your town and needs assistance.

After establishing these obligations, Allah ﷻ adds a critical qualifier: وَلَا تُبَذِّرْ تَبْذِيرًا - “And do not spread wastefully.” The word تَبْذِير comes from the image of a farmer carelessly throwing seeds around. Just as that farmer doesn’t realize where the seeds are falling, a wasteful spender doesn’t track where their money goes - they’re just throwing it around, blowing money without thought or care.

What’s the connection between giving relatives their rights and wasteful spending? The money you’re throwing around carelessly was deserved by them first. Before you thought of spending it irresponsibly, it had a rightful claim on it.

The Quran identifies two types of wrongful spending. The first is إسراف - spending beyond legitimate means on something that is itself permissible. The thing is halal, but the amount is excessive. An $80 sandwich when a $10 meal would suffice. A $3,000 suit when reasonable clothing is available. A $200,000 car when reliable transportation costs far less. These limits aren’t rigidly defined by Shariah with exact percentages - it’s a conscience call that requires honest self-reflection. Not everyone is on the same financial scale, but everyone must ask themselves: Is this excessive for my situation?

The second type is تَبْذِير - spending with no legitimate reason whatsoever, or spending on prohibited things. This includes entertainment that violates Islamic principles, extravagance that serves no purpose, and displays of wealth that have no benefit.

I’ve witnessed heartbreaking examples of this. Flying into a city for a Quran conference, I looked out the airplane window and saw a plane from a Gulf country with لا إله إلا الله written on it. I thought to myself, “La ilaha illallah” - but I don’t think they were coming for the Quran conference.

I once visited a Muslim household that threw their teenage son a graduation party with various forms of entertainment that had no place in a Muslim home. When I was asked to give them advice afterwards, the lady of the house handed me a check. “What’s this for?” I asked. “For your family,” she replied. She had this perception that anyone giving religious reminders must need charity. I had to refuse emphatically and then sit down to talk with her properly.

After some conversation, she began crying and asked me a question that revealed the depth of the problem: “What if you don’t want to go to Jannah?” I was stunned. “Why wouldn’t you want to go to Jannah?” She answered, “I love this house too much. I don’t want to leave it.” It was indeed a beautiful house - the dining table seated thirty, there was an artificial waterfall, a magnificent pool. But to reach that state of attachment, where this world completely eclipses the next - that’s the product of tabdheer.

Ayah 27

إِنَّ ٱلْمُبَذِّرِينَ كَانُوٓا۟ إِخْوَٰنَ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينِ ۖ وَكَانَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ لِرَبِّهِۦ كَفُورًا

Allah ﷻ now explains the spiritual reality behind wasteful spending: إِنَّ ٱلْمُبَذِّرِينَ كَانُوٓا۟ إِخْوَٰنَ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينِ - “Indeed, those who engage in wasteful spending have become the brothers of the devils.” This is followed by وَكَانَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ لِرَبِّهِۦ كَفُورًا - “And Shaytan has always been excessively disobedient, excessively ungrateful to his Master.”

Why does Allah ﷻ call wasteful spenders the brothers of Shaytan? One of Shaytan’s primary agendas, as mentioned elsewhere in the Quran, is to instill animosity among people. He does this through alcohol and gambling, but those are just two of his methods. Another powerful way he creates social discord is by encouraging the wealthy to flaunt their riches, building hatred and resentment among those who have less. A class-based society emerges, dividing people who should be united.

I’ve seen this in places like Pakistan, where a village leader will throw an extravagant wedding for his daughter - decorating the entire village, serving fresh food to thousands, employing three hundred workers just for the event. Meanwhile, every one of those employees has multiple daughters of their own whom they cannot afford to marry. That one wedding could have gotten the entire village married and then some. How can those employees not feel resentment? This is exactly the social poison Shaytan wants to spread: إِنَّمَا يُرِيدُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ أَن يُوقِعَ بَيْنَكُمُ ٱلْعَدَٰوَةَ - “Shaytan only wants to instill animosity among you.”

Ayah 28

وَإِمَّا تُعْرِضَنَّ عَنْهُمُ ٱبْتِغَآءَ رَحْمَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّكَ تَرْجُوهَا فَقُل لَّهُمْ قَوْلًا مَّيْسُورًا

This ayah addresses a delicate situation. The conversation returns to close relatives. Perhaps they came to you asking for help - which is already an embarrassing situation for them. Or perhaps you yourself don’t spend wastefully, but you’re in a tight financial spot. What should you do if you have to turn them away?

وَإِمَّا تُعْرِضَنَّ عَنْهُمُ - “If you have to turn away from them” - meaning if you must ignore or cannot acknowledge their request - ٱبْتِغَآءَ رَحْمَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّكَ تَرْجُوهَا - “because you are in your own pursuit of mercy from your Master that you are hoping for.” In other words, you’re in difficulty yourself, hoping Allah ﷻ will show you mercy.

In this situation, فَقُل لَّهُمْ قَوْلًا مَّيْسُورًا - “Then speak to them with a word that is easy.” Let them down gently. Sit them down, give them hope. Say something like, “I’m in the same situation as you. Let’s both pray and trust in Allah ﷻ. I don’t have it either. I’m in trouble myself.” Don’t slam the door in their face. Make it an opportunity to share hope with them.

There’s another dimension to this ayah: if you yourself are in pursuit of Allah’s mercy that you’re hopeful for, then the way to guarantee yourself that mercy is to let them off easy. The path to receiving Allah’s mercy includes speaking to others with softness, especially when delivering difficult news.

Ayah 29

وَلَا تَجْعَلْ يَدَكَ مَغْلُولَةً إِلَىٰ عُنُقِكَ وَلَا تَبْسُطْهَا كُلَّ ٱلْبَسْطِ فَتَقْعُدَ مَلُومًا مَّحْسُورًا

Allah ﷻ now provides guidance on balanced financial behavior using vivid imagery. وَلَا تَجْعَلْ يَدَكَ مَغْلُولَةً إِلَىٰ عُنُقِكَ - “Do not make your hand chained to your neck.” In Arabic, when they say someone’s hand is tied or chained, it’s describing extreme stinginess.

Imagine your hand is literally tied to your neck. Now you’re at the cash register and need to reach into your pocket for money. What happens? You’d have to choke yourself to get your hand down. The image describes someone who has such difficulty spending that every time they reach for their wallet, they feel like they’re being strangled. Don’t be that miserly, that cheap.

But then comes the other extreme: وَلَا تَبْسُطْهَا كُلَّ ٱلْبَسْطِ - “And do not extend your hand completely open as far as it can go.” Picture someone walking with their hands fully extended out to the sides. If you walked like that through a grocery store aisle, what would happen? You’d be grabbing everything, putting it all in your cart. This imagery represents getting your hands into financial matters they don’t belong in, making purchases you shouldn’t make, entering transactions you have no business entering.

The ayah applies not just to shopping but also to charity. You might get emotional and think, “I’m going to be like the great companions! I’ll sell my house and give it all away!” But wait - do you have a plan after that? Abu Bakr (RA) gave everything he had, but he was still in business afterwards. He still had income. He had a plan.

It’s unfair to take the top rank of the thousands of companions - whose iman on one side of the scale outweighs the iman of the entire ummah on the other side - and use them as the minimal standard for everyone. They are absolutely our role models, and we should strive to emulate their behavior. But you cannot take isolated accounts and say, “I’m going to do exactly that” without being responsible about your own situation.

Why this caution? فَتَقْعُدَ مَلُومًا مَّحْسُورًا - “So that you end up sitting there blameworthy and filled with regret.” If you give charity emotionally and then regret it afterwards, that charity may not be accepted. Give within your means, thoughtfully. Don’t give because you were emotionally manipulated at a fundraiser. Give to good causes because they are genuinely good causes and because you are able to.

Muslims should be financially mature enough to recognize that institutions like the masjid provide tremendous service to the community. Regardless of whether you agree with every administrative decision or program, the blessings that come to a community through the existence of the masjid are undeniable. As a matter of fairness, you should give back. This should be part of your planned giving, not an emotional reaction.

At the same time, it’s inappropriate for religious institutions to emotionally manipulate people by suggesting their iman is in jeopardy if they don’t give, or that they won’t make it to Jannah. That’s spiritually questionable. It takes away respect for the ayahs, for the hadith, and for the profound legacies of the companions (RA).

Ayah 30

إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَبْسُطُ ٱلرِّزْقَ لِمَن يَشَآءُ وَيَقْدِرُ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ بِعِبَادِهِۦ خَبِيرًۢا بَصِيرًا

This ayah beautifully ties the subject together. إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَبْسُطُ ٱلرِّزْقَ لِمَن يَشَآءُ - “Indeed, your Master is the One Who expands provision for whom He wills” - وَيَقْدِرُ - “and He restricts it.”

When you help someone financially, you might start thinking, “I helped them, so they should be okay now.” But it’s not up to you. It’s not your giving that expands anyone’s provision. Perhaps twenty or thirty days later, they find themselves in the same difficulty. You might think, “But I helped you! That should have solved everything. What happened?”

Allah ﷻ is teaching us: He is the One Who expands provision. He is the One Who restricts it. You’re not in charge of outcomes. Your responsibility is to give when you’re able and when it’s appropriate. The results are in Allah’s hands.

إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ بِعِبَادِهِۦ خَبِيرًۢا بَصِيرًا - “Indeed, He has always been, especially when it comes to His slaves, fully informed and in full view.” Allah ﷻ knows the reality of each person’s situation. He knows who genuinely needs help and who doesn’t. He knows who will use assistance wisely and who won’t. He sees everything about His servants’ circumstances. When we give, we do so with limited knowledge. Allah ﷻ distributes provision with perfect knowledge and perfect wisdom.

Ayah 31

وَلَا تَقْتُلُوٓا۟ أَوْلَـٰدَكُمْ خَشْيَةَ إِمْلَـٰقٍ ۖ نَّحْنُ نَرْزُقُهُمْ وَإِيَّاكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ قَتْلَهُمْ كَانَ خِطْـًٔا كَبِيرًا

Speaking of financial responsibility, Allah ﷻ addresses a heinous crime: وَلَا تَقْتُلُوٓا۟ أَوْلَـٰدَكُمْ خَشْيَةَ إِمْلَـٰقٍ - “Do not kill your children out of fear of poverty.”

The word إِمْلَـٰق is significant. It doesn’t mean poverty that already exists - it means the danger or fear of future poverty. The root meaning involves the destruction or loss of wealth. So this is about fears regarding the future: “If we have children, we won’t be able to afford them. Our wealth will be destroyed.”

This ayah has profound relevance today. Issues of abortion, contraception and birth control are being debated worldwide. Many countries and environmental groups advocate for population control, claiming the world’s population is growing too fast. But there’s a solution to legitimate concerns about population growth: marriage. The rate of illegitimate births is actually increasing faster than births within marriage. Promote nikah, promote the sense of responsibility that comes with maintaining a household, and you’ll see real population management. When people understand the responsibility involved, that naturally affects their decisions.

The debates about contraception and abortion aren’t just Muslim issues anymore - these are global concerns. The Quran offers solutions not just for Muslims but for all of humanity. Notice that this ayah doesn’t restrict its audience - يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ (O humanity) appears throughout the Quran. These are universal principles.

Allah ﷻ responds to the fear directly: نَّحْنُ نَرْزُقُهُمْ وَإِيَّاكُمْ - “We provide for them and for you.” Notice the order: the children are mentioned first in the provision because the fear is associated with providing for children in the future. Allah ﷻ says, “We provide for them” - addressing the fear directly - “and We provide for you as well.”

The ayah concludes with stark clarity: إِنَّ قَتْلَهُمْ كَانَ خِطْـًٔا كَبِيرًا - “Indeed, their killing has always been an enormous mistake, a huge error.” The word خِطْـًٔا indicates a grave mistake, an error of tremendous magnitude. There is no justification, no excuse that makes this acceptable. Financial fears about the future do not justify taking innocent life.

Ayah 32–36 · linked video: 08. Al-Isra’ (Ayah 32-36)

PRIMARY_AYAH: 32

Ayah 32

وَلَا تَقْرَبُوا۟ ٱلزِّنَىٰٓ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ فَـٰحِشَةً وَسَآءَ سَبِيلًا

The Quran addresses two major crimes against the individual—murder and fornication (zina)—and interestingly, these two are mentioned interchangeably throughout the Quran. In some passages, fornication is mentioned first, then murder. In others, the order is reversed. This is because fornication represents a spiritual death, while murder represents physical death. Both are capital crimes of profound seriousness. One ends your physical existence; the other kills your soul.

The language Allah ﷻ uses here is particularly striking: وَلَا تَقْرَبُوا - “do not even go near.” He doesn’t simply say “do not commit fornication,” which would be the more obvious prohibition. Instead, He warns us not to even approach it. This is because fornication is a trap you won’t realize you’ve fallen into until you’re already too deep.

The progression is subtle and dangerous. It starts innocently—“We’re just talking,” “We’re just working on a project together,” “We’re just chatting once in a while.” Before you know it, boundaries have been crossed. I’ve heard countless variations: “There’s a really good sister at the MSA…” or “There’s this coworker who keeps asking me questions about Islam—is it okay if I take him out for coffee to discuss Islam?”

The reality check needed here is simple: Has the entire male population suddenly disappeared that this particular sister needs your specific guidance? Is there no one else who can answer his questions about Islam? Have some common sense. And inevitably, two months later: “Brother, you know that guy? He’s thinking about becoming Muslim, but he wants to marry me first.” This shouldn’t be a surprise to anyone paying attention.

Our religion draws clear boundaries for good reason. You must control yourself and restrict yourself. This entire discussion in the surah began with taking care of family—parents and extended relatives. When family bonds are strong, you develop respect for the family unit. When you respect family, you understand what it means when someone speaks inappropriately to one of your sisters or cousins. That protective instinct should extend to all families. When you see any woman, you don’t just see her as an individual disconnected from context—you recognize she’s somebody’s daughter, somebody’s sister, potentially somebody’s wife. This awareness keeps you in control.

This powerful teaching of Islam is being lost. The common response is: “Brother, it sounds like you’re saying I can’t control myself. I can handle it. It’s no big deal for me.” But remember the story of Adam and Eve (AS). Allah ﷻ told them, “Do not go near this tree, lest you become wrongdoers.” He didn’t say, “Don’t eat from it after careful consideration.” He said don’t approach it, because once you get close, the next step happens almost automatically. It’s like entering Earth’s gravitational field—once you’re in range, gravity does the rest.

Many Muslim cultures developed protective practices inspired by this Islamic principle. Speaking to the girl’s family, having parents meet first, establishing proper channels of communication—these aren’t necessarily explicit shari’ah rulings, but they’re inspired by the command وَلَا تَقْرَبُوا (don’t even go near it). They exist to protect us by finding safer, more respectful approaches.

The common question: “What’s wrong with dating? How can I marry someone I haven’t even gotten to know?” reveals a fundamental misunderstanding of marriage. You barely know your spouse after ten years of marriage. People present what they want you to see in public settings. Their real self emerges when you live together, when life’s pressures test you both. The idea that a few coffee dates or conversations reveal someone’s true character is delusional. I’ve witnessed numerous cases of Muslim couples who were “seeing each other” before marriage who ended up in miserable marriages, constantly telling each other, “I thought you were someone else.”

The ayah concludes emphatically: إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ فَـٰحِشَةً - “Indeed, it has always been a shameless thing.” This isn’t a cultural taboo that changes with time or place. It’s not a social construct that will eventually phase out. It has always been wrong, from the beginning of humanity. وَسَآءَ سَبِيلًا - “And what a horrible path it is.” The path itself leads to destruction, affecting not just individuals but entire societies.

Ayah 33

وَلَا تَقْتُلُوا۟ ٱلنَّفْسَ ٱلَّتِى حَرَّمَ ٱللَّهُ إِلَّا بِٱلْحَقِّ ۗ وَمَن قُتِلَ مَظْلُومًا فَقَدْ جَعَلْنَا لِوَلِيِّهِۦ سُلْطَـٰنًا فَلَا يُسْرِف فِّى ٱلْقَتْلِ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ مَنصُورًا

The logical continuation from fornication to murder is profound. Murder is mentioned twice in this section of the surah—once regarding the sanctity of family (the prohibition against killing children), and now in connection with sexual morality. When a society normalizes fornication, something devastating happens: you stop seeing another human being as a soul that Allah ﷻ has honored. Instead, you reduce them to mere flesh, an object for gratification. The value of human life plummets when fornication increases in society. When human life is devalued, what crime becomes more likely? Murder. The crime of fornication degrades human beings, and once degraded, taking a life becomes less significant. They even give it sanitized names now—“crimes of passion”—as though that somehow lessens the heinousness.

وَلَا تَقْتُلُوا۟ ٱلنَّفْسَ ٱلَّتِى حَرَّمَ ٱللَّهُ - “Do not kill the soul which Allah has made sacred.” The word حَرَّمَ (sanctified, made sacred) is crucial because the previous ayah about avoiding fornication ensures the dignity of the human being. Murder violates that divinely ordained dignity. The exception given is إِلَّا بِٱلْحَقِّ - “except by right”—referring to due process through legitimate judicial systems, court trials, and proper legal procedures.

The ayah then addresses the victim’s family: وَمَن قُتِلَ مَظْلُومًا فَقَدْ جَعَلْنَا لِوَلِيِّهِۦ سُلْطَـٰنًا - “Whoever is killed unjustly, We have given his guardian authority.” This authority must be exercised responsibly: فَلَا يُسْرِف فِّى ٱلْقَتْلِ - “He should not go overboard in killing.” The reference here is to pre-Islamic tribal practices where one murder could spark a cycle of revenge wiping out entire tribes. Islam establishes limits and due process.

إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ مَنصُورًا - “Indeed, he will be aided.” The victim’s family has the support of the state, the society, and the Islamic judicial system. The system favors the victim, giving the victim’s family the right to determine the punishment, whether capital punishment, financial compensation, or pardon. It doesn’t allow a jury or prosecutor to make deals behind closed doors. This centers justice on those who have been wronged.

Ayah 34

وَلَا تَقْرَبُوا۟ مَالَ ٱلْيَتِيمِ إِلَّا بِٱلَّتِى هِىَ أَحْسَنُ حَتَّىٰ يَبْلُغَ أَشُدَّهُۥ ۚ وَأَوْفُوا۟ بِٱلْعَهْدِ ۖ إِنَّ ٱلْعَهْدَ كَانَ مَسْـُٔولًا

Now the discussion shifts to financial conduct and integrity. وَلَا تَقْرَبُوا۟ مَالَ ٱلْيَتِيمِ - “Do not go near the wealth of the orphan.” Notice again the use of تَقْرَبُوا (go near), the same language used for fornication. Certain boundaries must not even be approached. The orphan represents the most vulnerable in society, someone without a natural protector. Their wealth is particularly susceptible to exploitation.

The exception: إِلَّا بِٱلَّتِى هِىَ أَحْسَنُ - “except in a way that is better.” This refers to managing or investing the orphan’s wealth in a manner that genuinely benefits them, acting as a trustworthy guardian until حَتَّىٰ يَبْلُغَ أَشُدَّهُۥ - “he reaches his maturity.”

The ayah then shifts to a broader principle: وَأَوْفُوا۟ بِٱلْعَهْدِ - “Fulfill the promise.” Why is promise-keeping specifically highlighted after mentioning the orphan? There’s wisdom in this connection. When you have authority over someone—like an orphan—you’re less inclined to fulfill promises made to them. A parent tells their child, “I’ll take you to the zoo tomorrow, I promise,” but tomorrow comes and suddenly it was just a joke. Why? Because the parent has ultimate authority. Who’s going to hold them accountable? Similarly, someone in a position of power over an orphan might think, “Who will question me if I don’t follow through?”

Allah ﷻ makes clear: you have an Authority above you. إِنَّ ٱلْعَهْدَ كَانَ مَسْـُٔولًا - “Indeed, the promise will be questioned.” Who will ask about it? Allah ﷻ Himself will interrogate you about every promise made, regardless of the power dynamics involved.

Ayah 35

وَأَوْفُوا۟ ٱلْكَيْلَ إِذَا كِلْتُمْ وَزِنُوا۟ بِٱلْقِسْطَاسِ ٱلْمُسْتَقِيمِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ خَيْرٌ وَأَحْسَنُ تَأْوِيلًا

وَأَوْفُوا۟ ٱلْكَيْلَ إِذَا كِلْتُمْ - “Fulfill the measure when you measure.” The word كَيْل is beautifully comprehensive. It refers to measuring weight, measuring size, and even abstract measurements like time. In modern terms, this covers both products and services. Whether you’re selling goods or operating under a contract—a six-month agreement, a 40-hour work week, a specific timeframe—you must fulfill what you promised. If a product must be delivered by a certain time with certain quality standards, meet those standards. If you agreed to provide a service for a particular rate, honor that agreement.

I know someone who worked in a factory in Pakistan that manufactures medical equipment for export to the West. When he took over management, he discovered workers were packaging products regardless of quality—broken items, improperly made gauze, materials still wet—and shipping them anyway. He insisted they meet the agreed-upon standards, and people looked at him like he was crazy. “You’re not going to make money that way.” His response: “I don’t care about profit if it means cheating. We have to answer to Allah ﷻ.”

This mindset is widespread. The milk vendor waters down the milk. At work, you don’t fulfill your contract properly. You delay payments after agreeing to terms. Two months after agreeing to a price, you come back and say, “Well, the market changed, the price went up.” No—you agreed. That’s final. You can’t go back and pressure someone because they’re in a difficult position.

وَزِنُوا۟ بِٱلْقِسْطَاسِ ٱلْمُسْتَقِيمِ - “And weigh with a scale that is absolutely straight.” قِسْطَاس is the tool that ensures fairness and accuracy. Make certain there are no irregularities in your measurements or dealings.

The conclusion is remarkable: ذَٰلِكَ خَيْرٌ - “That is better.” Honest business practices are better for you. But Allah ﷻ adds: وَأَحْسَنُ تَأْوِيلًا - “and better in final outcome.” While تَأْوِيل commonly means interpretation, its literal meaning relates to bringing something to its ultimate conclusion or consequence. Honest dealings are better immediately, but also in the long run. Your customers return. Your reputation remains intact. You become known for integrity. This is the deeper, lasting benefit.

Ayah 36

وَلَا تَقْفُ مَا لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلسَّمْعَ وَٱلْبَصَرَ وَٱلْفُؤَادَ كُلُّ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ كَانَ عَنْهُ مَسْـُٔولًا

وَلَا تَقْفُ مَا لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ - “Do not follow what you have no knowledge of.” The verb قَفَا means to get right behind someone, to follow so closely you’re almost touching their back. Allah ﷻ commands us not to blindly follow anything without knowledge supporting it.

This is one of the deepest ayahs in the surah. As Muslims, we’re told to base our actions on knowledge. Knowledge comes in two forms: revealed knowledge (from Allah ﷻ) and acquired knowledge (what we learn through observation and study). When Adam (AS) was taught the names of all things, he was being equipped to acquire knowledge on earth—history, science, agriculture, various disciplines. This knowledge continues to expand.

But we also have revealed knowledge. The emphasis here, as the ayah continues, relates particularly to acquired knowledge and sound reasoning. إِنَّ ٱلسَّمْعَ وَٱلْبَصَرَ وَٱلْفُؤَادَ - “Indeed, the hearing, the sight, and the heart.” Hearing and seeing are the primary tools of acquiring knowledge about the world. Don’t follow superstitions. Don’t follow baseless claims. “Birds are a bad omen”—where did you get that from? We create elaborate superstitions: you can’t get married in certain months, you must keep a Quran in your car’s dashboard for protection, if you recite a particular verse fifty times you’ll never get a speeding ticket.

These fabrications have no basis in knowledge. Someone might think, “I can handle myself, I’m not superstitious,” but then make decisions based on feelings rather than facts. There are two drivers in human behavior: knowledge and reason on one hand, emotion on the other. This ayah calls us not to be swayed by emotion alone.

Consider how emotion dominates even the most sophisticated societies. In economics, “People are feeling nervous”—and the stock market plummets, billions of dollars lost. In politics, emotional rhetoric about a single issue can make or break entire campaigns. One statement, one gaffe gets amplified emotionally, and suddenly all actual information and reasoning go out the window.

Allah ﷻ is telling us to build a society of reason, not pure emotion. You don’t put feelings first; you use your ability to listen carefully (السَّمْع), to observe accurately (الْبَصَر), and to analyze properly with sound reasoning. The word فُؤَاد (heart) has two interconnected meanings. One refers to the heart that’s inflamed with emotion. The other meaning relates to the intellect at its highest form—like food that’s been fully cooked (the root connects to completion and processing). It’s the faculty that processes information and reaches sound conclusions.

When people act from overwhelming emotion, they justify their behavior: “I wasn’t myself. I was upset when I said that. You can’t hold me responsible.” The ayah’s conclusion demolishes this excuse: كُلُّ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ كَانَ عَنْهُ مَسْـُٔولًا - “All of those will be questioned about it.” You cannot stand before Allah ﷻ and say, “That’s just how I am when I’m angry,” or “I can’t help myself, that’s my nature.” You will be interrogated about your hearing, your sight, and your heart—both your emotions and your reasoning. Every faculty you possess will be held accountable.

Ayah 37–52 · linked video: 09. Al-Isra’ (Ayah 37-52)

Surah Al-Isra: Ayahs 37-52

Ayah 37

وَلَا تَمْشِ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مَرَحًا ۖ إِنَّكَ لَن تَخْرِقَ ٱلْأَرْضَ وَلَن تَبْلُغَ ٱلْجِبَالَ طُولًا

After addressing the importance of knowledge, Allah ﷻ now tackles the danger that often accompanies knowledge: arrogance. Knowledge is a double-edged sword, and with it comes the risk of pride.

“Do not walk upon the earth exultantly.” The word مَرَحًا represents the third and highest degree of prideful joy, an arrogant display of self-satisfaction. Allah ﷻ reminds us of our place: “Indeed, you will never tear the earth, and you will never reach the mountains in height.”

The imagery is powerful. When someone walks with arrogance, stomping on the earth as if they own it, Allah ﷻ asks them: Who do you think you are? You cannot cause even a crack in the earth beneath your feet. The Arabic word خَرْقَ refers to a piece of cloth being ripped off, emphasizing how insignificant our impact is despite our inflated sense of importance. Nor can we reach the heights of the mountains, no matter how tall we imagine ourselves to be. This ayah humbles us, reminding us to know our place in creation.

Ayah 38

كُلُّ ذَٰلِكَ كَانَ سَيِّئُهُۥ عِندَ رَبِّكَ مَكْرُوهًا

“All that - its evil is ever, in the sight of your Lord, detested.” This ayah refers back to all the prohibitions mentioned in the preceding passages. Each of these acts, including the arrogance just described, has always been evil in the sight of your Master, and they have always been detestable.

The word مَكْرُوهًا in the Quran carries stronger weight than in fiqh terminology. When something is described as makruh in the Quran, it means Allah ﷻ despises it - it is unacceptable to Him. We should not confuse this with the technical term used in Islamic jurisprudence where makruh refers to something merely disliked but permissible.

Ayah 39

ذَٰلِكَ مِمَّآ أَوْحَىٰٓ إِلَيْكَ رَبُّكَ مِنَ ٱلْحِكْمَةِ ۗ وَلَا تَجْعَلْ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَـٰهًا ءَاخَرَ فَتُلْقَىٰ فِى جَهَنَّمَ مَلُومًا مَّدْحُورًا

“That is from what your Lord has revealed to you of wisdom.” Allah ﷻ identifies these teachings as wisdom - divine guidance that has been revealed before and continues to be revealed.

Then comes a stark warning: “And do not make with Allah another deity, lest you be thrown into Hell, blamed and banished.” This echoes the very first revelation given to Musa (AS): worship none but Allah ﷻ. The warning applies universally, and notice that Allah ﷻ directs this severe language even toward His final Messenger ﷺ - not to insult him, but to demonstrate that the warnings of Allah ﷻ apply equally to all His slaves. The standards are not different for the Messenger ﷺ and different for the rest of us. The same warnings apply across the board.

When you have a true relationship with Allah ﷻ as Master, your priority becomes doing what He wants. But many people end up in a compromised relationship with Allah ﷻ - they give Him the weekend, a little charity here, some prayer there, but insist the rest is “my life.” This attitude leads to various forms of shirk, including the outrageous claims about Allah ﷻ having children.

Ayah 40

أَفَأَصْفَىٰكُمْ رَبُّكُم بِٱلْبَنِينَ وَٱتَّخَذَ مِنَ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ إِنَـٰثًا ۚ إِنَّكُمْ لَتَقُولُونَ قَوْلًا عَظِيمًا

Allah ﷻ exposes the absurdity of the mushrikun’s beliefs with biting irony: “Then, has your Lord chosen you for sons and taken from among the angels daughters? Indeed, you say a grave saying.”

The Arabs despised daughters, burying them alive as we read earlier in this surah. Yet they attributed daughters to Allah ﷻ, claiming the angels were His daughters. Allah ﷻ challenges this twisted logic: So somehow your Lord in His purity chose sons for you - because you prefer sons - and took daughters for Himself? You hate daughters so much that you bury them alive, yet what you hate for yourselves, you assign to Allah ﷻ?

This is not merely shirk - it is shirk committed in the most insulting way possible. They give Allah ﷻ their leftovers, what they consider worthless. “Indeed, you say a grave saying” - you are uttering something enormously wrong.

Ayah 41

وَلَقَدْ صَرَّفْنَا فِى هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانِ لِيَذَّكَّرُوا۟ وَمَا يَزِيدُهُمْ إِلَّا نُفُورًا

If you haven’t noticed, Surah Al-Isra mentions the Quran more than any other surah. The word “Quran” appears repeatedly throughout this passage.

“And We have certainly diversified in this Quran that they might be reminded.” The word صَرَّفْنَا means We have explained things in multiple ways, alternating approaches, presenting the message from different angles - all so they might make an effort to remember and understand.

“But it increases them not except in aversion.” Despite all these varied explanations and approaches, it has not increased them in anything except نُفُورًا - more hatred, more aversion. The word nufur relates to a military force that goes out against the enemy driven by hatred and hostility.

Ayah 42

قُل لَّوْ كَانَ مَعَهُۥٓ ءَالِهَةٌ كَمَا يَقُولُونَ إِذًا لَّٱبْتَغَوْا۟ إِلَىٰ ذِى ٱلْعَرْشِ سَبِيلًا

“Say, ‘If there had been with Him other gods as they say, then they would have sought to the Owner of the Throne a way.’”

This ayah presents a logical argument against polytheism. Allah ﷻ reveals a pattern found across mythologies - whether Greek, Hindu, or others - without the Prophet ﷺ having studied any of them. In every polytheistic system, you find conflict among the gods. There’s always a struggle for supremacy, with lesser gods plotting against the supreme deity, trying to overthrow him or take his throne.

Allah ﷻ says: If there were multiple gods, they would pursue a path to the One who possesses the Throne. Just as in any kingdom, you have princes, nephews, and sons all waiting for the king to die or plotting to eliminate their rivals to claim the throne next. The divine realm, if it operated like they imagine, would be full of this same drama and intrigue. Each god would be scheming to become the ultimate authority.

Ayah 43

سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ وَتَعَـٰلَىٰ عَمَّا يَقُولُونَ عُلُوًّا كَبِيرًا

“Exalted is He and high above what they say by great sublimity.”

Allah ﷻ is far too perfect, far too elevated to be described in such ways. He is utterly above these attributions of rivalry, conflict, and limitation. عُلُوًّا كَبِيرًا - He is above in a height that is enormous, in a transcendence that is beyond our comprehension.

Ayah 44

تُسَبِّحُ لَهُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتُ ٱلسَّبْعُ وَٱلْأَرْضُ وَمَن فِيهِنَّ ۚ وَإِن مِّن شَىْءٍ إِلَّا يُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِهِۦ وَلَـٰكِن لَّا تَفْقَهُونَ تَسْبِيحَهُمْ ۗ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ حَلِيمًا غَفُورًا

“The seven heavens and the earth and whatever is in them exalt Him.” Notice the continuous present tense - تُسَبِّحُ - they are declaring His perfection constantly and will continue to do so forever.

“And there is not a thing except that it exalts by His praise.” The ayah could have used مَا, but instead uses إِن with من - a grammatical choice that removes any possibility of debate. This is not a response to someone’s objection; it is a statement of absolute fact. You are being informed, educated about the reality of continuous glorification happening everywhere in creation.

“But you do not understand their exalting.” Everything in existence is engaged in tasbih, in declaring Allah’s perfection. Obviously, who is failing miserably in this mission? Human beings. Yet despite the entire universe being on board while we lag behind, Allah ﷻ describes Himself as حَلِيمًا غَفُورًا - forbearing and forgiving.

Halim carries an element of patience, like the patience a mother shows toward a disobedient child. The entire universe declares His perfection, yet humanity rebels - and still He remains forbearing and forgiving toward us.

Ayah 45

وَإِذَا قَرَأْتَ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ جَعَلْنَا بَيْنَكَ وَبَيْنَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ حِجَابًا مَّسْتُورًا

“And when you recite the Quran, We place between you and those who do not believe in the Hereafter a concealed partition.”

Notice the specific description: those who do not believe in the afterlife. As the surah progresses into its second half, you’ll see this becomes the central issue. What’s particularly interesting is that this section addresses both the mushrikun and the People of the Book - specifically the Jews. They share something significant: both groups effectively eliminated belief in the afterlife from their religions.

This requires clarification. Musa (AS) was certainly taught about the afterlife - among the very first teachings he received on the mountain was “Indeed, the Hour is coming.” But if you study Jewish literature, the afterlife has become a faded, nearly absent concept. It has been systematically removed.

Whenever the Messenger ﷺ recites the Quran, Allah ﷻ places a حِجَابًا - a barrier - between him and the disbelievers. A hijab is any obstruction that blocks view or access, whether a curtain, wall, or partition. But notice the description: مَّسْتُورًا - concealed, covered up.

Mastur comes from satr, which means to cover, but with a subtle difference from hijab. While hijab completely blocks, satr covers almost entirely but not completely - there may be small gaps. Here, the barrier itself is mostly invisible. Some signs of the barrier are available - the Messenger ﷺ can detect certain obstacles - but for the most part, it’s a spiritual reality that remains hidden. The barrier exists, but they cannot perceive it.

Ayah 46

وَجَعَلْنَا عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ أَكِنَّةً أَن يَفْقَهُوهُ وَفِىٓ ءَاذَانِهِمْ وَقْرًا ۚ وَإِذَا ذَكَرْتَ رَبَّكَ فِى ٱلْقُرْءَانِ وَحْدَهُۥ وَلَّوْا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَدْبَـٰرِهِمْ نُفُورًا

Now we encounter multiple words describing barriers. “And We have placed over their hearts coverings, lest they understand it.” The word أَكِنَّةً (akinnatan) refers to a shade that keeps something from the sun. We saw another form of this word earlier when Allah ﷻ mentioned placing secure coverings over mountains.

The imagery is profound: We have placed upon their hearts a seal that prevents light from reaching them. Just as a kinnan keeps something shaded from the sun, these coverings keep their hearts in darkness. The light of revelation, the light of guidance, cannot penetrate to illuminate their hearts. Had that light reached them, they would have understood.

“And in their ears deafness.” وَقْرًا describes a burden or blockage in the ear. When someone has a sinus infection, their ears feel filled up, creating a barrier where sound cannot pass through properly. Every time they swallow, they can hear the blockage. It’s like a mute button has been pressed - sound is completely shut off. This is what Allah ﷻ describes: a burden, a load placed inside their ears preventing them from hearing the truth.

“And when you mention your Lord alone in the Quran, they turn back in aversion.” When the Messenger ﷺ mentions Allah ﷻ as وَحْدَهُۥ - the One and Only - they become annoyed. Why does he only talk about Allah ﷻ? Why doesn’t he mention our other deities? We have so many gods, yet he refuses to acknowledge any of them.

This attitude manifests in modern times as well, even among some Muslim circles. When someone focuses on teaching about Allah ﷻ and the Quran, you’ll hear complaints: “How come they don’t talk about the awliya? How come they don’t mention special figures whose blessings we can access? Why do they only talk about the Prophet ﷺ? Isn’t there anyone else in our history worth discussing?”

The issue is not that there’s no one else in our history worthy of mention. The scholars, the righteous predecessors - they have their place. But when people start feeling this strong need for other sources, it becomes a psychological crutch. They want intermediaries who will somehow be the reason for their salvation. They think: We’ll make dua through their blessings, and it will all work out.

Leaving aside the theological implications, the psychological problem is clear: such people don’t want to take personal responsibility. The Quran doesn’t allow that escape. When you mention your Lord alone in the Quran, وَلَّوْا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَدْبَـٰرِهِمْ نُفُورًا - they turn on their backs immediately, fleeing out of hatred and aversion.

Ayah 47

نَّحْنُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا يَسْتَمِعُونَ بِهِۦٓ إِذْ يَسْتَمِعُونَ إِلَيْكَ وَإِذْ هُمْ نَجْوَىٰٓ إِذْ يَقُولُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ إِن تَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا رَجُلًا مَّسْحُورًا

“We are most knowing of how they listen when they listen to you.” Allah ﷻ knows exactly what they’re listening to, how valuable and relevant these words are that they’re choosing to ignore. We know better than anyone what is being presented to them and what they’re rejecting. When they act like His words are nothing, Allah ﷻ says: I know precisely what you’re dismissing.

“And when they are in private conversation.” The word نَجْوَىٰٓ means secret counsel or conspiracy. Interestingly, it shares a root with najat, which means rescue or salvation. Why? Because when people conspire, they’re usually trying to save themselves from someone or something. We need to get rid of that person; we want to save ourselves from the problem he represents. That’s the nature of najwa.

The word najwa can function as both a verbal noun and a noun of place. Here it describes their secret meetings where they plot against the message.

“When the wrongdoers say, ‘You follow not but a man affected by magic.’” Picture this scene: They’re in their secret meeting, conspiring against the Messenger ﷺ. Then one of them says, “You know, I heard the Quran today, and it was really deep. I know we oppose this man, but those were some heavy ayahs.”

The others realize one of their conspirators is beginning to give in to the Word of Allah ﷻ. They must dismantle that immediately and bring him back in line - or rather, back out of line. So they quickly respond: “You’re only following a man who’s been possessed by magic! He’s spellbound! Don’t be impressed - it was just magic. Don’t let it affect you.”

Ayah 48

ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ ضَرَبُوا۟ لَكَ ٱلْأَمْثَالَ فَضَلُّوا۟ فَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ سَبِيلًا

“Look how they strike for you comparisons; but they have strayed, so they cannot find a way.”

Allah ﷻ tells the Messenger ﷺ - not so he can see it, but so that Allah ﷻ Himself can point out - look at how they talk about you. Watch how they try to give examples to figure you out, to categorize you, to explain you away. They’re trying so hard to understand what’s happening with you, O Messenger.

فَضَلُّوا۟ - then they went astray. فَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ سَبِيلًا - they are not even capable of finding a path. Because they have insulted the Messenger of Allah ﷺ, because they have rejected the ayahs of Allah ﷻ, they have lost their way completely. They cannot find any path forward.

Ayah 49

وَقَالُوٓا۟ أَءِذَا كُنَّا عِظَـٰمًا وَرُفَـٰتًا أَءِنَّا لَمَبْعُوثُونَ خَلْقًا جَدِيدًا

“And they say, ‘When we are bones and crumbled particles, will we indeed be resurrected as a new creation?’”

Their mockery continues. When we’ve been reduced to nothing but bones, when we’ve become رُفَـٰتًا (rufatan) - fragments scattered and mixed with dirt, completely decomposed - are we really going to be raised again as a new creation? The tone is one of utter disbelief and ridicule.

Allah ﷻ has responded to this type of question before in the Quran. He’s given analogies about plants emerging from the soil, about the One who created them the first time having no difficulty creating them again. But they keep coming back as if they’ve never heard any response, asking the same question with the same mocking tone.

Now Allah ﷻ has had enough. There comes a point where He draws a line and says: Know your place.

Ayah 50

۞ قُلْ كُونُوا۟ حِجَارَةً أَوْ حَدِيدًا

“Say, ‘Be stones or iron.’”

This is Allah’s response through His Messenger ﷺ, and it’s beautifully sharp. They’re asking condescendingly: “How can bones and dust be brought back to life?”

The response: Bones and dust? You’re worried about bones and dust? Tell them to become something with no potential for life whatsoever. Become rocks. Become iron. These materials never produce life. Soil can take in seeds and raise plants - there’s potential there. But stones? Iron? There’s no life in them at all, no organic potential.

It’s as if the Messenger ﷺ is saying: Go ahead, try turning into rocks and iron. Make it as hard as you can imagine for Allah ﷻ, and then He will still bring you back. And then you’ll burn. This is Allah’s divine way of telling them: Enough. Your mockery means nothing.

Ayah 51

أَوْ خَلْقًا مِّمَّا يَكْبُرُ فِى صُدُورِكُمْ ۚ فَسَيَقُولُونَ مَن يُعِيدُنَا ۖ قُلِ ٱلَّذِى فَطَرَكُمْ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ ۚ فَسَيُنْغِضُونَ إِلَيْكَ رُءُوسَهُمْ وَيَقُولُونَ مَتَىٰ هُوَ ۖ قُلْ عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَكُونَ قَرِيبًا

“Or be a creation of that which is great within your breasts.” Use your imagination - become whatever you consider the hardest thing to recreate, whatever seems most impossible to bring back to life in your minds.

“And they will say, ‘Who will restore us?’” Notice they don’t even ask “Will we be brought back?” anymore. Now it’s “Who could possibly do this?”

“Say, ‘He who created you the first time.’” The One who fashioned you, who molded you from nothing - الَّذِى فَطَرَكُمْ - the first time around.

“Then they will nod their heads toward you and say, ‘When is that?’” Picture this: فَسَيُنْغِضُونَ إِلَيْكَ رُءُوسَهُمْ - they’ll start bobbing their heads up and down. The verb يُنْغِضُونَ specifically means to move the head up and down in that nodding motion. “Oh yeah, oh right, the One who made us the first time. Uh-huh, we heard that before.”

They’re acknowledging it sarcastically, going through the motions. And then they ask mockingly: “When is it coming? When is this Day you keep threatening us with?”

“Say, ‘Perhaps it will be soon.’” عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَكُونَ قَرِيبًا - Maybe it’s very near. Maybe it’s closer than you think.

Ayah 52

يَوْمَ يَدْعُوكُمْ فَتَسْتَجِيبُونَ بِحَمْدِهِۦ وَتَظُنُّونَ إِن لَّبِثْتُمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا

“On the Day He will call you and you will respond with praise of Him.”

You obnoxious bunch who are making fun of this conversation, mocking the idea of resurrection - there will come a Day when He calls you. And on that Day, فَتَسْتَجِيبُونَ بِحَمْدِهِۦ - you will be desperately trying your best to respond to Him by praising Him. “SubhanAllah! You’re so perfect! We’re so sorry! Please forgive us!”

All that arrogance, all that mockery will evaporate instantly. You’ll be scrambling to declare His perfection, trying to make up for a lifetime of rebellion in a single moment.

“And you will think you had not remained except a little.” وَتَظُنُّونَ إِن لَّبِثْتُمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا - at that moment, you will be absolutely convinced that you didn’t spend much time in the world at all. Your entire life, which seemed so long, so important, so permanent - you’ll realize you remained in the earth except very little. All those years of arrogance and denial will feel like they passed in the blink of an eye.

Ayah 53–67 · linked video: 10. Al-Isra’ (Ayah 53-67)

Surah Al-Isra: Ayahs 53-67

Ayah 53

وَقُل لِّعِبَادِى يَقُولُوا۟ ٱلَّتِى هِىَ أَحْسَنُ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنَ يَنزَغُ بَيْنَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنَ كَانَ لِلْإِنسَـٰنِ عَدُوًّا مُّبِينًا

This ayah addresses a crucial aspect of Islamic conduct: choosing better speech even when provoked. The context is significant—the disbelievers were saying obnoxious things directly to the Prophet ﷺ, statements that would naturally provoke anger. Imagine how upset hearing such things would make anyone, especially when directed at the Messenger of Allah ﷺ himself.

When we become angry, we often say things we later regret. The Arabs had expressions for this: “Whoever follows his anger ends up losing his mannerisms” and “The beginning of anger is insanity, followed by regret.” This is why Allah ﷻ instructs His Prophet ﷺ to tell the believers: “Say that which is better.”

Notice the precision in the word choice. Allah ﷻ uses أَحْسَنُ, meaning “better,” not “best.” The best speech has already been revealed—it’s the word of Allah ﷻ. Human beings cannot produce the absolute best. But we can always choose the better option in our responses. When someone speaks in a foul way, we should find a better way to respond. When communicating with each other, we should pause before speaking and ask ourselves: Is there a less offensive way to convey this point?

This principle applies universally in our daily interactions. Even in moments when sarcasm or clever comebacks tempt us, we must exercise restraint. The smartest response isn’t always the best response. Sometimes holding back requires real effort, but it’s essential for maintaining harmony.

The ayah then explains the consequence of not choosing better speech: إِنَّ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنَ يَنزَغُ بَيْنَهُمْ—Shaytan creates friction between people. The word يَنزَغُ describes the friction Shaytan generates between parties. It’s not just our words that cause problems; Shaytan takes those words, adds his own distortion, and presents them to others in the worst possible light. They then interpret the words negatively and respond accordingly.

This is why we should avoid language that carries dual meanings—words that could be taken positively or negatively. Tone, gesture, and context all affect interpretation. Someone with a history of using sarcasm finds it hard to be taken literally because people assume they mean the opposite of what they say.

The ayah concludes with a powerful reminder: إِنَّ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنَ كَانَ لِلْإِنسَـٰنِ عَدُوًّا مُّبِينًا—He has always been an open enemy to the human being. Shaytan wants to create friction among us. Throughout the Quran, we learn he does this through various means: alcohol, gambling, gossip, and now through bad language. The common thread? He seeks to instill hatred and animosity among people.

Ayah 54

رَّبُّكُمْ أَعْلَمُ بِكُمْ ۖ إِن يَشَأْ يَرْحَمْكُمْ أَوْ إِن يَشَأْ يُعَذِّبْكُمْ ۚ وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ عَلَيْهِمْ وَكِيلًا

Your Master knows you better than anyone else. If He wills, He will show you mercy. If He wills, He will punish you. The absolute authority over these outcomes belongs to Allah ﷻ alone.

Then comes the critical statement to the Prophet ﷺ: وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ عَلَيْهِمْ وَكِيلًا—We have not sent you as one who will take care of all their affairs. The word وَكِيلًا refers to someone who manages all matters on behalf of others. The disbelievers cannot simply hand their responsibilities over to the Prophet ﷺ and expect him to handle everything. They remain accountable for their own choices and actions.

Ayah 55

وَرَبُّكَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَلَقَدْ فَضَّلْنَا بَعْضَ ٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ ۖ وَءَاتَيْنَا دَاوُۥدَ زَبُورًا

This ayah addresses a specific context involving the Jews and Christians who began comparing prophets after hearing the Quran reference previous revelations and Jewish history. They started asking: Who’s better? Who ranks higher? The Prophet ﷺ himself warned the Companions (RA) in a famous hadith not to compare him with other prophets, specifically mentioning Prophet Yunus (AS).

This instruction seems puzzling at first. After all, this very surah mentions the Maqam Mahmud, the Praised Station where the Prophet ﷺ will lead all other prophets in prayer. So why can’t we make such comparisons? Because these discussions shift focus away from Allah ﷻ and turn religion into a competition—my team versus your team. It becomes a distraction from the essence of faith itself.

Allah ﷻ declares: Your Master knows better whoever is in the heavens and the earth. Then He states: وَلَقَدْ فَضَّلْنَا بَعْضَ ٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ—We gave preference to some prophets over others. Notice the deliberate ambiguity. Allah ﷻ could have specified ranks: number one, number two, top ten, top twenty. Instead, He left it general. We gave some preference over others—that’s it. Only Allah ﷻ knows their exact ranking.

To help the Jews understand this isn’t about putting them down, Allah ﷻ adds: وَءَاتَيْنَا دَاوُۥدَ زَبُورًا—We gave Dawud the Zabur. This beautiful revelation was his special honor. You don’t need to worry about ranking him at position 47 or 48. This isn’t a playoff system or competition. That ranking belongs solely to Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 56

قُلِ ٱدْعُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ زَعَمْتُم مِّن دُونِهِۦ فَلَا يَمْلِكُونَ كَشْفَ ٱلضُّرِّ عَنكُمْ وَلَا تَحْوِيلًا

This ayah powerfully brings the conversation back to focus after the distraction. Tell them to call upon those you have placed so much confidence in besides Allah ﷻ. فَلَا يَمْلِكُونَ كَشْفَ ٱلضُّرِّ عَنكُمْ—They don’t possess the authority to alleviate harm from you. كَشْف means the alleviation or removal of harm. وَلَا تَحْوِيلًا—Nor do they have the power to change anything in your situation at all.

The problem with the conversation about which prophet is better is that these people were calling on prophets as though they were objects of worship, as though they could relieve their problems. This is the fundamental error being addressed.

Ayah 57

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ يَبْتَغُونَ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِمُ ٱلْوَسِيلَةَ أَيُّهُمْ أَقْرَبُ وَيَرْجُونَ رَحْمَتَهُۥ وَيَخَافُونَ عَذَابَهُۥٓ ۚ إِنَّ عَذَابَ رَبِّكَ كَانَ مَحْذُورًا

Now Allah ﷻ reveals the reality of those prophets themselves—the very ones people make into objects of worship. Those you call upon are themselves pursuing الْوَسِيلَةَ, a means of getting closer to their Master. Earlier in this surah, Allah ﷻ commanded: seek means to get closer to Him.

What were the lives of the prophets made of? Constantly calling upon Allah ﷻ, constantly finding ways to draw nearer to Him. أَيُّهُمْ أَقْرَبُ—Which of them will get closer? The only competition they thought about among themselves was: How can I get closer to Allah ﷻ? Their competition wasn’t with each other—it was focused entirely on their Lord.

وَيَرْجُونَ رَحْمَتَهُۥ—They hope for His mercy. وَيَخَافُونَ عَذَابَهُۥٓ—And they fear His punishment. These prophets, with all their elevated stations, lived between hope and fear of Allah ﷻ.

إِنَّ عَذَابَ رَبِّكَ كَانَ مَحْذُورًا—The punishment of your Master has always been something warned about. The word مَحْذُورًا means cautioned, something to take extreme caution about. The addition of كَانَ (“has always been”) is significant. It tells us that all previous revelations warned about Allah’s punishment. This is particularly important because one group—the Jews—had practically eliminated this concept from their religious practice. Yet every revelation has always contained that warning.

Ayah 58

وَإِن مِّن قَرْيَةٍ إِلَّا نَحْنُ مُهْلِكُوهَا قَبْلَ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ أَوْ مُعَذِّبُوهَا عَذَابًا شَدِيدًا ۚ كَانَ ذَٰلِكَ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مَسْطُورًا

There is not a single town except that We will destroy it before the Day of Resurrection, or punish it with severe punishment. كَانَ ذَٰلِكَ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مَسْطُورًا—This has always been written, documented line by line in the Book. مَسْطُورًا means lined, penciled into the book.

No nation will survive eventual destruction. We know nations deserve punishment when they reject truth, but how that punishment comes—whether through natural disaster, war, internal collapse, or other means—remains part of Allah’s unseen knowledge. The decree is certain; the manner and timing belong to His wisdom alone.

Ayah 59

وَمَا مَنَعَنَآ أَن نُّرْسِلَ بِٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ إِلَّآ أَن كَذَّبَ بِهَا ٱلْأَوَّلُونَ ۚ وَءَاتَيْنَا ثَمُودَ ٱلنَّاقَةَ مُبْصِرَةً فَظَلَمُوا۟ بِهَا ۚ وَمَا نُرْسِلُ بِٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ إِلَّا تَخْوِيفًا

The Quraysh moved to their next argument: “Show us something we can see.” The Jews had actually encouraged them to make this demand—to ask for a physical miraculous sign. Allah ﷻ responds: What prevented Us from sending miraculous signs except that the earliest ones already lied against them? Previous nations received clear miracles, yet those signs did them no good because they rejected them anyway.

وَءَاتَيْنَا ثَمُودَ ٱلنَّاقَةَ مُبْصِرَةً—And We gave Thamud the she-camel, clearly visible. Here’s an example close to the Arabs’ own history. You’re asking for a visible sign? The people of Thamud received one that was super visible—a miraculous she-camel. فَظَلَمُوا۟ بِهَا—Then they wronged against that sign. They killed the camel and brought destruction upon themselves.

وَمَا نُرْسِلُ بِٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ إِلَّا تَخْوِيفًا—We don’t send Our miraculous signs except as a means of instilling fear, as warning. Now, revelation itself serves this purpose. If you ask for something beyond it, the only thing that will be sent is something that should instill fear in you.

Ayah 60

وَإِذْ قُلْنَا لَكَ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ أَحَاطَ بِٱلنَّاسِ ۚ وَمَا جَعَلْنَا ٱلرُّءْيَا ٱلَّتِىٓ أَرَيْنَـٰكَ إِلَّا فِتْنَةً لِّلنَّاسِ وَٱلشَّجَرَةَ ٱلْمَلْعُونَةَ فِى ٱلْقُرْءَانِ ۚ وَنُخَوِّفُهُمْ فَمَا يَزِيدُهُمْ إِلَّا طُغْيَـٰنًا كَبِيرًا

Allah ﷻ now warns the Quraysh: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ أَحَاطَ بِٱلنَّاسِ—Your Master has surrounded people. He has them completely encompassed. What could be scarier than being surrounded with no escape? Yet even this warning wouldn’t move them to take the message seriously.

وَمَا جَعَلْنَا ٱلرُّءْيَا ٱلَّتِىٓ أَرَيْنَـٰكَ إِلَّا فِتْنَةً لِّلنَّاسِ—We didn’t make the vision that We showed you except as a trial for people. This refers to Al-Isra wal-Mi’raj, the Night Journey and Ascension. When the Prophet ﷺ told people he had traveled to Jerusalem and ascended to the seventh heaven, some people’s iman increased tremendously. Others became convinced they would never accept Islam—it became a trial that pushed them further into disbelief.

وَٱلشَّجَرَةَ ٱلْمَلْعُونَةَ فِى ٱلْقُرْءَانِ—And the cursed tree in the Quran—the tree of Zaqqum. When this is mentioned, people mock it. “What kind of tree grows in fire? Doesn’t it need water? How come wood doesn’t burn?” They manufacture counter-questions to ridicule the concept.

Whether Allah ﷻ says He has them surrounded, or describes the miraculous journey, or mentions the tree of Zaqqum—their response is the same. وَنُخَوِّفُهُمْ فَمَا يَزِيدُهُمْ إِلَّا طُغْيَـٰنًا كَبِيرًا—We try to instill fear in them, but it increases them in nothing except enormous rebellion. طُغْيَـٰنًا كَبِيرًا—huge rebellion is the only thing that gets added.

Ayah 61

وَإِذْ قُلْنَا لِلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ ٱسْجُدُوا۟ لِـَٔادَمَ فَسَجَدُوٓا۟ إِلَّآ إِبْلِيسَ قَالَ ءَأَسْجُدُ لِمَنْ خَلَقْتَ طِينًا

There’s a quick transition here. The previous ayah ended with طُغْيَـٰنًا كَبِيرًا—huge rebellion. Where did huge rebellion begin? With Iblis. The Quraysh are now following in those footsteps.

When We said to all the angels: Prostrate before Adam, they all prostrated except Iblis. He said: “Should I prostrate before someone You created from clay?”

His mention that Adam (AS) was created from clay highlights Iblis’s arrogance. By extension, Allah ﷻ is highlighting the arrogance of the Quraysh. This is the reason they’re not accepting the message—they’re full of themselves, just as Iblis was, and they come up with similar obnoxious criticisms.

Ayah 62

قَالَ أَرَءَيْتَكَ هَـٰذَا ٱلَّذِى كَرَّمْتَ عَلَىَّ لَئِنْ أَخَّرْتَنِ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ لَأَحْتَنِكَنَّ ذُرِّيَّتَهُۥٓ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا

Iblis says: أَرَءَيْتَكَ—Do you see? This phrase comes from رأي (to see), but when used in this shocking, confrontational manner, it means: “Do you actually see? Do you really see this thing?”

هَـٰذَا ٱلَّذِى كَرَّمْتَ عَلَىَّ—This one You honored over me? Who is he referring to? Adam (AS).

لَئِنْ أَخَّرْتَنِ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ—If You just give me a little extra time until the Day of Resurrection… لَأَحْتَنِكَنَّ ذُرِّيَّتَهُۥٓ—I will utterly consume his offspring.

The word احتنك is fascinating. When locusts or insects destroy a crop overnight—when a swarm comes and eats away the entire field, leaving nothing good—Arabs use the verb حنك. Iblis uses this same verb to describe what he’ll do with humanity: “I will eat away all the good they have in them, so all that’s left is the worthless.”

إِلَّا قَلِيلًا—Except very little. Even Iblis acknowledges he won’t succeed with everyone. A small group will remain protected.

Ayah 63

قَالَ ٱذْهَبْ فَمَن تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ فَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ جَزَآؤُكُمْ جَزَآءً مَّوْفُورًا

Allah ﷻ responds: Go. Whoever follows you among them, then Jahannam is your compensation—yours and theirs together. Allah ﷻ addresses Iblis and his future human followers in the second person, as though the decree is already sealed.

جَزَآءً مَّوْفُورًا—A compensation that will be absolutely fulfilled. The word موفور comes from وفر, meaning to be filled, to overflow, more than necessary, beyond what’s required. You won’t be given any discount in the punishment. It will be filled to the brim and beyond.

Ayah 64

وَٱسْتَفْزِزْ مَنِ ٱسْتَطَعْتَ مِنْهُم بِصَوْتِكَ وَأَجْلِبْ عَلَيْهِم بِخَيْلِكَ وَرَجِلِكَ وَشَارِكْهُمْ فِى ٱلْأَمْوَٰلِ وَٱلْأَوْلَـٰدِ وَعِدْهُمْ ۚ وَمَا يَعِدُهُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ إِلَّا غُرُورًا

This is a powerful ayah where Allah ﷻ tells Iblis what his strategy will be—because Allah ﷻ already knows it.

وَٱسْتَفْزِزْ—This word استفزز is remarkable. It comes from the root فز. When someone intimidates you with their language and makes you move from your position—like when you’re standing somewhere and someone yells “Hey! What are you doing here? Get out! Move!”—and you initially hold your ground but eventually give way, that’s fazz.

The word is also used when a baby calf or goat is born and tries to stand for the first time with wobbly legs that can’t hold steady. That unsteadiness, that inability to maintain a firm stance, is fazz.

استفزز means to try to make somebody wobbly in their stance, to make them move from their position. People are on the straight path, and Shaytan comes to slip them, to weaken their footing. The opposite is found in our dua: ثَبِّتْ أَقْدَامَنَا—Make our feet firm. Shaytan wants استفزاز; we ask Allah ﷻ for تثبيت.

Go ahead and make slip whoever you can بِصَوْتِكَ—with your intimidating voice, with your whisper.

وَأَجْلِبْ عَلَيْهِم بِخَيْلِكَ وَرَجِلِكَ—March your cavalry and your foot soldiers against them. أَجْلِبْ means to march horses forward. خَيْل are riding soldiers; رَجِل (the plural of راجل) are foot soldiers. Shaytan has multitudes of followers—go ahead and release them against humanity.

وَشَارِكْهُمْ فِى ٱلْأَمْوَٰلِ وَٱلْأَوْلَـٰدِ—Participate with them in wealth and children. Teach them how to make haram money. Corrupt them in how they acquire and spend wealth, and in matters concerning their children.

وَعِدْهُمْ—And promise them all kinds of things. وَمَا يَعِدُهُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ إِلَّا غُرُورًا—Whatever Shaytan promises them is nothing but deception.

Ayah 65

إِنَّ عِبَادِى لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطَـٰنٌ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِرَبِّكَ وَكِيلًا

The previous ayah makes it sound like Shaytan has been given tremendous authority—an army to deploy, promises to make, strategies to execute. It sounds frightening, as though he wields real power.

Then Allah ﷻ immediately clarifies the reality: إِنَّ عِبَادِى لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطَـٰنٌ—My slaves, you have absolutely no authority over them whatsoever. He hasn’t actually been given much authority at all. His power is limited to whispers and deception. Over those who are truly Allah’s slaves, he has no control.

وَكَفَىٰ بِرَبِّكَ وَكِيلًا—Your Master is enough as one to take care of all your affairs. Remember the word وَكِيل from earlier? Here it returns. Allah ﷻ is the true Wakeel, the one who manages and protects His servants.

Ayah 66

رَّبُّكُمُ ٱلَّذِى يُزْجِى لَكُمُ ٱلْفُلْكَ فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ لِتَبْتَغُوا۟ مِن فَضْلِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ بِكُمْ رَحِيمًا

Allah ﷻ now reminds us of His care through a powerful everyday example. رَّبُّكُمُ ٱلَّذِى يُزْجِى لَكُمُ ٱلْفُلْكَ فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ—Your Master is the One who pushes the ships for you in the ocean.

The word يُزْجِى is beautiful. It means to push something to make it move. When an object doesn’t move on its own, you push it and it moves—that’s زجا. The ship is moving through the ocean, and we’re standing inside a cruise liner or ferry boat, watching it move. We might think it’s the motor, the engine, the technology. But Allah ﷻ reminds us: I am pushing it. Your Master is the one actively moving that ship through the water.

لِتَبْتَغُوا۟ مِن فَضْلِهِۦٓ—So that you can pursue and seek out His favor. The ability to travel, to trade, to explore, to seek opportunities—all of this is من فضله, from His bounty.

إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ بِكُمْ رَحِيمًا—No doubt He has been merciful to you. The use of كَانَ emphasizes this has always been His nature toward you.

Ayah 67

وَإِذَا مَسَّكُمُ ٱلضُّرُّ فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ ضَلَّ مَن تَدْعُونَ إِلَّآ إِيَّاهُ ۖ فَلَمَّا نَجَّىٰكُمْ إِلَى ٱلْبَرِّ أَعْرَضْتُمْ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ كَفُورًا

The previous ayah described Allah ﷻ pushing the ship in the ocean. Now let’s talk about what happens when trouble strikes in that same ocean.

وَإِذَا مَسَّكُمُ ٱلضُّرُّ فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ—When harm starts hitting you in the ocean, when the waves begin to slap against you… ضَلَّ مَن تَدْعُونَ—all the false gods you were calling upon, they literally get lost. You can’t find them. Their names don’t even come to your tongue.

إِلَّآ إِيَّاهُ—Except Him alone. In moments of true desperation, when death seems imminent, only Allah ﷻ remains. The natural human fitrah breaks through all the false beliefs and calls out to the One True God.

فَلَمَّا نَجَّىٰكُمْ إِلَى ٱلْبَرِّ أَعْرَضْتُمْ—But when He rescues you to the land, you begin ignoring again. You return to your state of deliberate, willful ignorance, as though that moment of desperate sincerity never happened.

وَكَانَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ كَفُورًا—The human being has always been extremely ungrateful. كَفُورًا is an intensive form, indicating extreme, persistent ingratitude. This has been the pattern throughout human history—moments of clarity in crisis, followed by forgetfulness in comfort.

This ayah serves as both a warning and an invitation to break this cycle, to maintain consciousness of Allah ﷻ not just in desperate moments at sea, but on the safety of dry land as well.

Ayah 68–82 · linked video: 11. Al-Isra’ (Ayah 68-82)

Surah Al-Isra: Ayahs 68-82

Ayah 68

أَفَأَمِنتُمْ أَن يَخْسِفَ بِكُمْ جَانِبَ ٱلْبَرِّ أَوْ يُرْسِلَ عَلَيْكُمْ حَاصِبًا ثُمَّ لَا تَجِدُوا۟ لَكُمْ وَكِيلًا

The scene shifts dramatically. You were just in the ocean, facing trouble, calling out desperately to Allah ﷻ. He saved you, and then what happened? You forgot it all again. Now where are you? Back on land, feeling secure once more.

Allah ﷻ asks: Do you feel safe now that He won’t cause you to sink right there on the edge of the land? The word خَسْف means to swallow up or cause to sink. Think you can only sink in the ocean? Allah ﷻ can sink you into the land just as easily. أَفَأَمِنتُمْ أَن يَخْسِفَ بِكُمْ جَانِبَ ٱلْبَرِّ – “Do you feel safe that He won’t swallow you at the edge of the land?”

Or consider this: أَوْ يُرْسِلَ عَلَيْكُمْ حَاصِبًا – “Or that He could send against you a shower of pebbles.” The word حَاصِب refers to a pebble, but also describes a meteor shower, rocks raining down from the sky by Allah’s command. ثُمَّ لَا تَجِدُوا۟ لَكُمْ وَكِيلًا – “Then you will not find anyone for yourselves to take care of your problem.”

Ayah 69

أَمْ أَمِنتُمْ أَن يُعِيدَكُمْ فِيهِ تَارَةً أُخْرَىٰ فَيُرْسِلَ عَلَيْكُمْ قَاصِفًا مِّنَ ٱلرِّيحِ فَيُغْرِقَكُم بِمَا كَفَرْتُمْ ۙ ثُمَّ لَا تَجِدُوا۟ لَكُمْ عَلَيْنَا بِهِۦ تَبِيعًا

The questioning continues: أَمْ أَمِنتُمْ أَن يُعِيدَكُمْ فِيهِ تَارَةً أُخْرَىٰ – “Or do you feel safe that He will not make you return to it another time?” You just got saved from the ocean and made it to land. But imagine an enemy approaches, or an earthquake strikes, or a volcano erupts. Where do you run? Back to the ship. Back to the very ocean you were just rescued from.

The word تَارَة is one of three Quranic terms for “turn” or “time.” Unlike مَرَّة, which indicates any occurrence, تَارَة specifically means doing something again after a while has passed. The Arabs would say, “Sometimes I do this, and other times I do that” using this word.

Do you feel that safe that Allah ﷻ won’t throw you back into the ocean a second time around? And if He does: فَيُرْسِلَ عَلَيْكُمْ قَاصِفًا مِّنَ ٱلرِّيحِ – “Then He would send upon you a storm made of wind.”

Notice the contrast. On land, what kind of punishment was mentioned? حَاصِبًا – rocks from the sky. Now in the ocean? قَاصِفًا – water damage. The root word refers to wood that has been hit by water so intensely and repeatedly that it becomes soft enough to break apart. A قَاصِف is a storm that destroys everything in its path through water, like a hurricane.

فَيُغْرِقَكُم بِمَا كَفَرْتُمْ – “Then it would drown you because of your disbelief.” ثُمَّ لَا تَجِدُوا۟ لَكُمْ عَلَيْنَا بِهِۦ تَبِيعًا – “Then you will not find against Us anyone to avenge you.” The word تَبِيع means a follower, someone who comes after to take revenge. When a nation’s ship is sunk, they send another army to avenge them. But who are you going to send to stand against Allah ﷻ?

Ayah 70

۞ وَلَقَدْ كَرَّمْنَا بَنِىٓ ءَادَمَ وَحَمَلْنَـٰهُمْ فِى ٱلْبَرِّ وَٱلْبَحْرِ وَرَزَقْنَـٰهُم مِّنَ ٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ وَفَضَّلْنَـٰهُمْ عَلَىٰ كَثِيرٍ مِّمَّنْ خَلَقْنَا تَفْضِيلًا

After describing these frightening scenarios, Allah ﷻ reminds humanity of His favor: وَلَقَدْ كَرَّمْنَا بَنِىٓ ءَادَمَ – “And We have certainly honored the children of Adam.” This is a powerful declaration of human dignity.

وَحَمَلْنَـٰهُمْ فِى ٱلْبَرِّ وَٱلْبَحْرِ – “And We carried them on the land and the sea.” The verb حَمَل is profound here. The fact that we can walk safely on the earth is itself a form of carrying. We feel this reality more acutely when we’re in the ocean, where we’re literally being carried. But Allah ﷻ is actually doing this for us in both places.

The word حَمَل is especially used for a mother carrying a child. When a mother carries her child, she is extremely careful because if she loosens her grip or becomes careless, the child will fall. This is the kind of protective care that Allah ﷻ describes in how He carries us on the earth. One little slip while you’re just walking, one moment of lost balance, and you could twist your foot the wrong way and suffer a spinal cord injury that leaves you paralyzed for life. Does it happen? Absolutely. Just last year in Plano, a 16-year-old barely touched a car that was pulling out of a driveway at one mile an hour. Just touched it lightly, and he fell in a way that ended his life. The cause of death was unknown, indescribable, inexplicable. He’s the One carrying you on land and sea.

وَرَزَقْنَـٰهُم مِّنَ ٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ – “And We provided them from the good and pure things.” وَفَضَّلْنَـٰهُمْ عَلَىٰ كَثِيرٍ مِّمَّنْ خَلَقْنَا تَفْضِيلًا – “And We gave them preference over so many of what We created, with all kinds of preference.”

We continue to discover new species on earth. We still haven’t catalogued all forms of life that exist on land or in the ocean. The more creations we find, the more we realize how much Allah ﷻ has given us preference over all of them.

Ayah 71
