يَوْمَ نَدْعُوا۟ كُلَّ أُنَاسٍۭ بِإِمَـٰمِهِمْ ۖ فَمَنْ أُوتِىَ كِتَـٰبَهُۥ بِيَمِينِهِۦ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يَقْرَءُونَ كِتَـٰبَهُمْ وَلَا يُظْلَمُونَ فَتِيلًا

يَوْمَ نَدْعُوا۟ كُلَّ أُنَاسٍۭ بِإِمَـٰمِهِمْ – “The Day We will call every group of people by their leaders, by their imams.”

Then comes a beautiful contrast: فَمَنْ أُوتِىَ كِتَـٰبَهُۥ بِيَمِينِهِۦ – “Then whoever is given his book in his right hand.” Consider the person given the book in his left hand. He faces two problems. First, it’s behind his back, so he cannot easily read it. Second, even if he could read it, he wouldn’t want to. He’s forced to read it: “Read your book!”

But notice what Allah ﷻ says about those given the book in the right hand: فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يَقْرَءُونَ كِتَـٰبَهُمْ – “Those will be reading their book.” They’re not told to read it. They’re doing it voluntarily, eagerly. “Oh my God, I got it in the right! What did I get? Page 87, my Hajj got accepted! I thought I messed up the slaughtering. I thought my haircut wasn’t good enough. But it got accepted!” They’re flipping through their record with joy.

وَلَا يُظْلَمُونَ فَتِيلًا – “And they will not be wronged even a fatil.” What is a فَتِيل? When you look at a date seed closely, you’ll notice a tiny thread or fiber coming out of it. That thread is called a fatil. They won’t be shortchanged even that much.

Why should you be happy about this? Because Allah ﷻ is really generous with the extra credit.

Ayah 72

وَمَن كَانَ فِى هَـٰذِهِۦٓ أَعْمَىٰ فَهُوَ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ أَعْمَىٰ وَأَضَلُّ سَبِيلًا

Why does Allah ﷻ suddenly mention blindness? The previous passages described things for us to see and observe. You’re traveling in the ocean, traveling on land. He’s protecting you through all of this. Don’t you see that? Are you blind to these signs?

وَمَن كَانَ فِى هَـٰذِهِۦٓ أَعْمَىٰ – “And whoever is blind in this life” – فَهُوَ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ أَعْمَىٰ – “he will be blind in the next life also” – وَأَضَلُّ سَبِيلًا – “and even more lost in terms of path.”

He’s lost now, but it’s nothing compared to the lostness he will experience when the Hereafter begins.

Ayah 73

وَإِن كَادُوا۟ لَيَفْتِنُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْكَ لِتَفْتَرِىَ عَلَيْنَا غَيْرَهُۥ ۖ وَإِذًا لَّٱتَّخَذُوكَ خَلِيلًا

The scene shifts to the Quraysh’s strategy with the Prophet ﷺ. وَإِن كَادُوا۟ لَيَفْتِنُونَكَ – “And they almost tried to tempt you, to put you on trial, to test and attack you” – عَنِ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْكَ – “away from what We have revealed to you.”

They would come to him saying, “We like you, Muhammad. We don’t have a problem with you personally. After all, you’re one of our brightest. It’s just this Quran thing. Just compromise a little. Give us a little. We ask you to show us something and you say no. That’s fine. But just accept a couple of our gods. At least mention them. It’s not that bad. We can work something out later.”

لِتَفْتَرِىَ عَلَيْنَا غَيْرَهُۥ – “So that you would fabricate against Us something other than it.” وَإِذًا لَّٱتَّخَذُوكَ خَلِيلًا – “And then they would take you as a very close friend.” You’d become their buddy if you just made a little change to the Quran.

They’d say, “How much longer will you do this? Don’t you think your life has become miserable? Haven’t you had enough? We want things to be better for you. We love you. You’re family. Just a little. Give us a little. Use your wisdom. Come on.”

This is the “good cop” strategy. They already tried the “bad cop” approach. Now they’re trying pressure through false friendship and apparent concern.

Ayah 74

وَلَوْلَآ أَن ثَبَّتْنَـٰكَ لَقَدْ كِدتَّ تَرْكَنُ إِلَيْهِمْ شَيْـًٔا قَلِيلًا

Allah ﷻ responds: وَلَوْلَآ أَن ثَبَّتْنَـٰكَ – “Had it not been that We made you firm” – لَقَدْ كِدتَّ تَرْكَنُ إِلَيْهِمْ شَيْـًٔا قَلِيلًا – “you might have possibly almost inclined toward them a little.”

Let me share something about the honor Allah ﷻ gives His Messenger ﷺ in this passage. Consider Yusuf (AS). When describing his potential error, Allah ﷻ said: وَلَقَدْ هَمَّتْ بِهِۦ ۖ وَهَمَّ بِهَا – “And she certainly intended [evil] toward him, and he would have intended toward her…” If you just read that part, it’s problematic. But immediately, the defense comes: لَوْلَآ أَن رَّءَا بُرْهَـٰنَ رَبِّهِۦ – “had he not seen the proof of his Lord.”

With Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, if the same sequence were followed, it would read: “You almost inclined toward them a little, had We not made you firm.” That order would give an inappropriate impression.

Out of extra vigilance for the honor of the Messenger ﷺ, Allah ﷻ began with His defense first, then mentioned what might have been possible. He didn’t even give Yusuf (AS) this level of precedence in the wording. But for the Messenger ﷺ, Allah ﷻ says: “No, no, no. I made you firm first. And had it not been for that, there was a possibility that you might have almost inclined.”

Look at all the layers removing any blame: “might have” (possibility, not certainty), “almost” (not actually), “inclined” (not fully compromised), and then شَيْـًٔا قَلِيلًا – “just a little,” barely, a negligible amount.

Ayah 75

إِذًا لَّأَذَقْنَـٰكَ ضِعْفَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ وَضِعْفَ ٱلْمَمَاتِ ثُمَّ لَا تَجِدُ لَكَ عَلَيْنَا نَصِيرًا

Even in this hypothetical scenario: إِذًا لَّأَذَقْنَـٰكَ ضِعْفَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ وَضِعْفَ ٱلْمَمَاتِ – “Then We would have made you taste double the punishment of worldly life and double the punishment of death.”

Who is Allah ﷻ talking to? His beloved Messenger ﷺ. The message is clear: even you, had you done even the slightest bit of compromise in this revelation, you would have been held in the highest position of accountability. No other person would be held to this standard.

ثُمَّ لَا تَجِدُ لَكَ عَلَيْنَا نَصِيرًا – “Then you would not find for yourself any aid against Us.” You would be standing against Allah ﷻ Himself.

Another honoring of the Messenger ﷺ: when you make someone taste something, it’s typically followed by mention of what they’re tasting. أَذَقْنَـٰكَ should be followed by “We would make you taste the punishment of…” But Allah ﷻ says ضِعْفَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ وَضِعْفَ ٱلْمَمَاتِ – “double of life and double of death.” The actual word “punishment” isn’t even mentioned. That’s also out of regard for the Messenger ﷺ. You can fill in the blank yourself. Allah ﷻ won’t say it directly because he’s addressing His Messenger ﷺ.

Ayah 76

وَإِن كَادُوا۟ لَيَسْتَفِزُّونَكَ مِنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ لِيُخْرِجُوكَ مِنْهَا ۖ وَإِذًا لَّا يَلْبَثُونَ خِلَـٰفَكَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا

وَإِن كَادُوا۟ لَيَسْتَفِزُّونَكَ مِنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ – “And it may very well be that they will try to make you slip from the land” – لِيُخْرِجُوكَ مِنْهَا – “so they can expel you from it.”

They’re trying to remove the Prophet’s ﷺ stability in Makkah. What is his stability? His uncle provides him protection and support. If that support disappears, his feet will become unsteady. They might be able to force him out.

وَإِذًا لَّا يَلْبَثُونَ خِلَـٰفَكَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا – “And if they do, they will not remain behind you except for a little while.” Did they eventually force him out of Makkah? Yes. These ayahs were revealed before the migration, yet Allah ﷻ already called it: if they kick you out, their own expulsion is coming soon after.

Eight years passed before the Muslims returned victoriously to Makkah. But for some of their biggest leaders, it didn’t even take eight years. Six months after the Prophet ﷺ moved to Madinah, Abu Jahl was gone at Badr. Can you imagine? The very people who expelled the Messenger ﷺ, within that same year, their reign was over. Seventy of the greatest leaders of Quraysh were annihilated.

Allah ﷻ called it before it happened.

Ayah 77

سُنَّةَ مَن قَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا قَبْلَكَ مِن رُّسُلِنَا ۖ وَلَا تَجِدُ لِسُنَّتِنَا تَحْوِيلًا

سُنَّةَ مَن قَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا قَبْلَكَ مِن رُّسُلِنَا – “This is the established way of those We have already sent before you from Our messengers.”

وَلَا تَجِدُ لِسُنَّتِنَا تَحْوِيلًا – “And you will not find any alteration, any change in Our way of dealing with things.” This is how Allah ﷻ has always dealt with those who oppose His messengers. His pattern doesn’t change.

Ayah 78

أَقِمِ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ لِدُلُوكِ ٱلشَّمْسِ إِلَىٰ غَسَقِ ٱلَّيْلِ وَقُرْءَانَ ٱلْفَجْرِ ۖ إِنَّ قُرْءَانَ ٱلْفَجْرِ كَانَ مَشْهُودًا

We just learned that they might kick you out of the land, and if they do, they won’t last long. Victory is on its way. So how should you prepare for that victory?

Don’t worry about the victory. Focus on what Allah ﷻ is telling you: أَقِمِ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ – “Establish the prayer.”

لِدُلُوكِ ٱلشَّمْسِ – “From the duluk of the sun.” The word دُلُوك means to get loose, to start descending. When the sun begins coming down from its zenith.

إِلَىٰ غَسَقِ ٱلَّيْلِ – “To the ghasaq of the night” – the darkest part of the night.

وَقُرْءَانَ ٱلْفَجْرِ – “And the Quran of Fajr.” Notice that the Quran uses قُرْءَان as the word for Salat Al-Fajr. Why? Because you’re supposed to recite a lot of Quran in Fajr. The recitation should be longer than in other prayers.

We should probably feel a bit guilty at this point. Many of us rush through Fajr with the shortest surahs possible.

إِنَّ قُرْءَانَ ٱلْفَجْرِ كَانَ مَشْهُودًا – “Indeed, the Quran of Fajr is witnessed.” Some narrations tell us that the angels change shifts at Fajr time. So how many groups of angels witness Fajr? Both shifts, the one coming in and the one going out. That’s why this prayer is most witnessed.

This is the ayah that essentially covers all five daily prayers. Even though only three time periods are mentioned directly, they encompass all five. From duluk (when the sun starts declining) to ghasaq (deep darkness), you have Dhuhr, Asr, Maghrib and Isha covered. Then Fajr is mentioned separately. Two time periods giving you five prayers.

Ayah 79

وَمِنَ ٱلَّيْلِ فَتَهَجَّدْ بِهِۦ نَافِلَةً لَّكَ عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَبْعَثَكَ رَبُّكَ مَقَامًا مَّحْمُودًا

وَمِنَ ٱلَّيْلِ فَتَهَجَّدْ بِهِۦ – “And in the depth of the night, stand in prayer vigilantly with it.”

نَافِلَةً لَّكَ – “As an additional obligation for you.” This is extra specifically for the Prophet ﷺ. He must do it. It’s not optional for him the way it is for us.

عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَبْعَثَكَ رَبُّكَ مَقَامًا مَّحْمُودًا – “Perhaps your Lord will raise you to a praised station.” Al-Maqam Al-Mahmud, the Station of Praise, is the elevated rank appointed to the Prophet ﷺ above all creation on Judgment Day.

That station will come one day. But right now, the Prophet ﷺ is in Makkah being humiliated. He doesn’t know what’s coming next. So Allah ﷻ tells him about a future that awaits him.

Ayah 80

وَقُل رَّبِّ أَدْخِلْنِى مُدْخَلَ صِدْقٍ وَأَخْرِجْنِى مُخْرَجَ صِدْقٍ وَٱجْعَل لِّى مِن لَّدُنكَ سُلْطَـٰنًا نَّصِيرًا

Allah ﷻ teaches the Prophet ﷺ a powerful supplication: وَقُل رَّبِّ أَدْخِلْنِى مُدْخَلَ صِدْقٍ – “And say: My Lord, enter me with an entrance of truth, a true entrance, an entrance of nobility and respect.”

Notice something beautiful here. The immediate concern is being expelled from Makkah, right? لِيُخْرِجُوكَ – they want to get you out. So if the concern is expulsion, logically which verb should come first? “Expel me” or “Enter me”?

If you’re getting expelled, the natural order would be: “Get me out in a noble way, and once I’m out, get me into a place that is noble.” Exit first, then entrance.

But the Prophet ﷺ is taught to say the opposite: أَدْخِلْنِى مُدْخَلَ صِدْقٍ وَأَخْرِجْنِى مُخْرَجَ صِدْقٍ – “Enter me with a true entrance, and bring me out with a true exit.”

Why this order? Because Allah ﷻ is teaching him: Don’t worry about being kicked out. Focus on what matters most. Ask first for where you’re going, not where you’re leaving from. When somebody is forced out of their home, what’s the bigger concern? That they lost their old home, or whether they’ll have a new one?

Allah ﷻ says: Ask for the new home first. The fulfillment of this dua is Madinah. “Enter me into a noble place” – that’s Madinah. “And when You take me out of Makkah, do it in a way that preserves my dignity, that makes it a true escape, not a shameful fleeing.”

وَٱجْعَل لِّى مِن لَّدُنكَ سُلْطَـٰنًا نَّصِيرًا – “And grant me from Yourself an authority that aids.” This is profound. سُلْطَان means authority, but paired with نَصِير (that which helps), it takes on deeper meaning.

Did the Prophet ﷺ receive authority in Madinah? Yes. But notice: he doesn’t just ask for سُلْطَان. He asks for سُلْطَـٰنًا نَّصِيرًا – helpful authority, authority that aids in completing the mission.

Someone can have authority without having a mission. But the Prophet ﷺ needs authority not for its own sake. He needs authority because he’s on a divine mission. So he asks for the kind of authority that will help him accomplish that mission.

Ayah 81

وَقُلْ جَآءَ ٱلْحَقُّ وَزَهَقَ ٱلْبَـٰطِلُ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلْبَـٰطِلَ كَانَ زَهُوقًا

All this conflict between Quraysh and the Prophet ﷺ is ongoing. The Jews are conspiring. Everything seems uncertain. And Allah ﷻ says: وَقُلْ جَآءَ ٱلْحَقُّ – “And say: Truth has already arrived.”

Don’t worry about it. Truth has already won.

وَزَهَقَ ٱلْبَـٰطِلُ – “And falsehood has vanished.” The verb زَهَقَ means to run off and disappear completely.

Imagine a championship boxing match, a fight to the death. The reigning champion walks into the stadium. The opponent sees him and just runs away in terror. That’s the image: جَآءَ ٱلْحَقُّ وَزَهَقَ ٱلْبَـٰطِلُ – truth arrived and falsehood ran off.

إِنَّ ٱلْبَـٰطِلَ كَانَ زَهُوقًا – “Indeed, falsehood was always meant to vanish.” It always does that. It always runs. It disappears completely. That’s its nature.

Ayah 82

وَنُنَزِّلُ مِنَ ٱلْقُرْءَانِ مَا هُوَ شِفَآءٌ وَرَحْمَةٌ لِّلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ۙ وَلَا يَزِيدُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ إِلَّا خَسَارًا

Allah ﷻ concludes this passage with a profound declaration about the Quran itself: وَنُنَزِّلُ مِنَ ٱلْقُرْءَانِ مَا هُوَ شِفَآءٌ وَرَحْمَةٌ لِّلْمُؤْمِنِينَ – “And We send down from the Quran that which is healing and mercy for the believers.”

The Quran isn’t here just to crush falsehood. It’s not meant to be an enemy. The door to the Quran’s healing and mercy remains open even to those who currently reject it.

وَلَا يَزِيدُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ إِلَّا خَسَارًا – “However, it does not increase the wrongdoers in anything except loss.”

The same Quran becomes healing and mercy for believers, while it becomes a source of trouble, pain and eventually loss and defeat for those who consciously wrong themselves by rejecting it. The choice of how to receive it lies with each person.

Ayah 83–102 · linked video: 12. Al-Isra’ (Ayah 83-102)

Surah Al-Isra: Ayahs 83-102

Ayah 83

وَإِذَآ أَنْعَمْنَا عَلَى ٱلْإِنسَـٰنِ أَعْرَضَ وَنَـَٔا بِجَانِبِهِۦ ۖ وَإِذَا مَسَّهُ ٱلشَّرُّ كَانَ يَـُٔوسًا

This ayah follows the previous discussion about the Quran having the power to heal, particularly the disease of what lies inside the chest. The biggest disease in the chest is arrogance, and Allah ﷻ now addresses this fundamental human flaw.

When We shower the human being with favor, he ignores it as though it’s not a favor at all. He becomes distant and turns to his side in pride. But when harm touches him, he becomes extremely depressed and loses hope entirely. This is the volatile pattern of human behavior when disconnected from gratitude and trust in Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 84

قُلْ كُلٌّ يَعْمَلُ عَلَىٰ شَاكِلَتِهِۦ فَرَبُّكُمْ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنْ هُوَ أَهْدَىٰ سَبِيلًا

Tell them: everyone works in accordance with their predisposition. The word شَاكِلَة comes from shakl, which means form or shape. Allah ﷻ created all our personalities within certain boundaries. There is a certain capability you have and certain limits you have. It’s important to know your capabilities and your limits. No matter what you do, you will not go beyond your limits, and everybody will work within their limits.

Some people will be able to do certain things very well, while other things they won’t be able to do as well. Just as our attitudes and abilities are different, our personalities are different. Everybody is going to work in accordance with what Allah ﷻ has gifted them with. This is why discovering what your shakilah is becomes essential—what has Allah ﷻ fashioned you with that you can serve the religion with?

At the end of the day, your nature is going to dominate. You won’t outgrow your skin. You are who you are. Your personalities and attitudes may be different, but the one thing you should all share in common is your pursuit of guidance. Your master knows who is the one that is more guided in terms of a path. Allah ﷻ knows who’s doing better in that pursuit.

Ayah 85

وَيَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلرُّوحِ ۖ قُلِ ٱلرُّوحُ مِنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّى وَمَآ أُوتِيتُم مِّنَ ٱلْعِلْمِ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا

Where do our differences come from? If we’re all created so differently and all of us have such different personalities, what’s the mysterious origin of that? Genetically and as far as creation is concerned, all of us are essentially organic matter, with the majority of our being made of water. Where is this uniqueness coming from?

They ask you about the soul—الرُّوحِ. The biggest mystery in psychology is personality, and the biggest mystery in our personality is the ruh itself. Tell them: the ruh is from the command of my Master. You haven’t been given out of knowledge except very, very little. You know very, very little about the ruh.

Our body and our physical being are from the world of khalq—creation. But the ruh is from the world of amr—command. The Angel Jibreel (AS) is also called ruh. The Quran is also called ruh. What we have been given inside of us is also called ruh. The means by which it is fed, the delivery mechanism, the food of it itself—all are termed ruh. Similarly, a word associated with this is nur—light. Revelation is called light. In hadith literature, angels are called creatures of light. But we still know very, very little about this creature, this thing called the ruh.

Ayah 86

وَلَئِن شِئْنَا لَنَذْهَبَنَّ بِٱلَّذِىٓ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْكَ ثُمَّ لَا تَجِدُ لَكَ بِهِۦ عَلَيْنَا وَكِيلًا

Had We wanted, We would remove, We would do away with what We have revealed to you—meaning the Quran. We wouldn’t give it to you. And then you wouldn’t find for yourself in its case, in the case of the Quran against Us, any caretaker. Nobody would be able to guard it. The only One preserving the Quran in your chest is Allah ﷻ Himself. If We wanted, it would be gone.

Ayah 87

إِلَّا رَحْمَةً مِّن رَّبِّكَ ۚ إِنَّ فَضْلَهُۥ كَانَ عَلَيْكَ كَبِيرًا

The revelation of the Quran is nothing but a mercy from your Master. The fact that you don’t forget it, that you continue to remember it and continue to progress in its preservation—this is a mercy from Allah ﷻ. No doubt, His favor on you, His blessing on you, certainly has been very huge.

What’s that blessing in the ayah? The revelation of the Quran. The fact that you received the Quran is a huge blessing. Notice the contrast here: they want you to change the Quran, but Allah ﷻ says the greatest thing you have is the Quran itself. That’s such a huge favor to you. It’s the contrary argument.

Ayah 88

قُل لَّئِنِ ٱجْتَمَعَتِ ٱلْإِنسُ وَٱلْجِنُّ عَلَىٰٓ أَن يَأْتُوا۟ بِمِثْلِ هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانِ لَا يَأْتُونَ بِمِثْلِهِۦ وَلَوْ كَانَ بَعْضُهُمْ لِبَعْضٍ ظَهِيرًا

Speaking of making changes to the Quran, here’s what you should tell them: If all of the human beings and jinn were together, that they would come forward to produce the likes of this Quran, they will not be producing anything like it—even if some of them were serving as backup to one another. Even if they got each other’s back, if they were helping each other, collaborating as best they could—if all of humanity collaborated to produce another Quran, they couldn’t.

Ayah 89

وَلَقَدْ صَرَّفْنَا لِلنَّاسِ فِى هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانِ مِن كُلِّ مَثَلٍ فَأَبَىٰٓ أَكْثَرُ ٱلنَّاسِ إِلَّا كُفُورًا

We have alternated, changed, presented in variations of ways for people in this Quran all kinds of examples. Yet people are just bent upon disbelief. They refuse any alternative but disbelief. أَبَىٰ means to refuse, to be bent upon something, to be stuck on something. People are stuck on disbelief and they won’t budge.

Ayah 90

وَقَالُوا۟ لَن نُّؤْمِنَ لَكَ حَتَّىٰ تَفْجُرَ لَنَا مِنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ يَنۢبُوعًا

Allah ﷻ now gives a sample case of how they’re stuck in their disbelief. Let Me tell you how they’re so stuck in their kufr. They would say: we will not believe you, we will not accept what you’re saying, until you bring forth, explode out of the ground for us, from the earth for us, a spring.

يَنۢبُوعًا —a spring of water from the ground that’s bubbling out—actually comes from the root letters ن ب ع. A spring of water that bursts from the ground in a crazy, bubbling manner, coming out of nowhere in the middle of the desert. How come you can’t produce that?

Ayah 91

أَوْ تَكُونَ لَكَ جَنَّةٌ مِّن نَّخِيلٍ وَعِنَبٍ فَتُفَجِّرَ ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرَ خِلَـٰلَهَا تَفْجِيرًا

Or how come you don’t own a garden that should be in your possession, made of palm trees and grapes? Then you should make rivers burst out from the middle of them, right through the middle—a huge river. It should be a huge explosion, and rivers should just crack through, so to speak.

Ayah 92

أَوْ تُسْقِطَ ٱلسَّمَآءَ كَمَا زَعَمْتَ عَلَيْنَا كِسَفًا أَوْ تَأْتِىَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ قَبِيلًا

Or maybe you should drop the sky on top of us as you see fit, piece by piece. كِسَفًا is a piece of something that’s soft, like cotton candy or fiber, that slowly falls to the ground. Why don’t you make the sky fall on us like that?

Or how about you bring Allah ﷻ and bring the angels along, قَبِيلًا —face to face? Qabila means face to face: “I saw him qabila—face to face.”

Ayah 93

أَوْ يَكُونَ لَكَ بَيْتٌ مِّن زُخْرُفٍ أَوْ تَرْقَىٰ فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَلَن نُّؤْمِنَ لِرُقِيِّكَ حَتَّىٰ تُنَزِّلَ عَلَيْنَا كِتَـٰبًا نَّقْرَؤُهُۥ ۗ قُلْ سُبْحَانَ رَبِّى هَلْ كُنتُ إِلَّا بَشَرًا رَّسُولًا

Or how about you should have a home you own made of gold? Or you should just start ascending into the sky—why don’t you just start rising, levitating into the sky? And we’re not going to believe in your ascension either. If we see you elevating into the sky, even that we won’t believe—until you come down with a book that we can read ourselves.

I’ve made a list of all of their nonsense, one by one. Tell them: سُبْحَانَ رَبِّى —how perfect is my Master! Am I anything but a mortal being that happens to be a messenger? بَشَرًا —like I told you before, this refers to skin. Am I anything more than that?

Ayah 94

وَمَا مَنَعَ ٱلنَّاسَ أَن يُؤْمِنُوٓا۟ إِذْ جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْهُدَىٰٓ إِلَّآ أَن قَالُوٓا۟ أَبَعَثَ ٱللَّهُ بَشَرًا رَّسُولًا

What forbade the people, what prevented them from believing when guidance had come to them? They would keep saying: “A human being is going to guide us? A human messenger is going to guide us?”

In other words, at the end of all their claims, the Messenger ﷺ said, “I’m just a human being.” Then they turned around and said, “So you’re just a human being—why should we follow you? Why are you any different from us? You go shopping like we do. You eat lunch and dinner like we do. You go to sleep when you need to. So why should we follow you?”

Allah ﷻ says this is the main thing that kept people from following: Why should we follow any other human being? The dominant human problem is accepting authority. We just don’t like authority. Why should I listen to anybody else? And the messenger demands to be listened to in a way that’s more strict and more absolute than any other entity. It’s very hard for someone who could argue they’re a free spirit, who doesn’t want to be enslaved, to accept that.

Ayah 95

قُل لَّوْ كَانَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مَلَـٰٓئِكَةٌ يَمْشُونَ مُطْمَئِنِّينَ لَنَزَّلْنَا عَلَيْهِم مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَلَكًا رَّسُولًا

Say: Had the population of the earth been angels, had the earth been filled with angels walking around all casually, calmed and relaxed, walking around in the park and having a picnic—then We would have sent an angel from the sky as a messenger.

What do you see? A population of angels around you that I should send you an angel as a messenger? I sent you a human messenger because you yourselves are human beings. If I sent you an angel, you would have said, “How can we follow him? He’s an angel. He’s so perfect. How can we follow his example? He was too up there.”

That’s actually exactly why we shouldn’t say that about the Prophet ﷺ. As perfect as he is in his character, he came so he could be followed—not so we praise him so much and say we can’t even be anything like him. We’re trying to be like the Messenger ﷺ. He came so you could try to be like him. Allah ﷻ didn’t send an angel for that exact reason, because then that excuse would exist.

To counterbalance what I was sharing with you about the Sahaba before: the Sahaba are to us in some sense superheroes, but not in the sense that they are beyond human. They were people just like us. They ate food and walked around and slept and had thoughts. They were just human beings like ourselves. So the great things they accomplished are not impossible for us. We shouldn’t think of them in that light. But at the same time, we shouldn’t take the highest of their accomplishments and only mention them as though that’s all they were—as though they weren’t human, as though they didn’t have other things happening in their lives.

Ayah 96

قُلْ كَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ شَهِيدًۢا بَيْنِى وَبَيْنَكُمْ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ بِعِبَادِهِۦ خَبِيرًۢا بَصِيرًا

Tell them: Allah ﷻ is enough as a witness between me and yourselves. My Quran has come. I have delivered my message, and I’m making sure Allah ﷻ will be the judge. He’s enough as a witness between me and yourselves. No doubt, He has been—especially when it comes to His slaves—in full knowledge. He is fully knowing, and He’s been in full view.

Ayah 97

وَمَن يَهْدِ ٱللَّهُ فَهُوَ ٱلْمُهْتَدِ ۖ وَمَن يُضْلِلْ فَلَن تَجِدَ لَهُمْ أَوْلِيَآءَ مِن دُونِهِۦ ۖ وَنَحْشُرُهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ عَلَىٰ وُجُوهِهِمْ عُمْيًا وَبُكْمًا وَصُمًّا ۖ مَّأْوَىٰهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ ۖ كُلَّمَا خَبَتْ زِدْنَـٰهُمْ سَعِيرًا

Whoever Allah ﷻ guides, then he will be the one committed to guidance. Who else would He guide except the one committed to guidance? And whoever He would mislead, you’re not going to find any protective friends other than Him to help him out.

We’re going to raise them on the Day of Resurrection on their faces—blind, mute and deaf. Why? Because they asked to see things but refused to see what was in front of them, so they lose their seeing. They refused to listen to this message and asked all these obnoxious questions, so they can’t speak anymore. They refused to listen to the advice when they could have benefited from it, so now it’s gone—they lose their hearing.

Their final place to go back to is Jahannam. Every time the fire starts running out of flame, starts drying down and dying down, We increase the flame. We increase for them, particularly, the flame. Jahannam is vast, and they may be in one corner of it, but the fire intensifies right where they are. We increase them in terms of the burning and the scorching.

Ayah 98

ذَٰلِكَ جَزَآؤُهُم بِأَنَّهُمْ كَفَرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَقَالُوٓا۟ أَءِذَا كُنَّا عِظَـٰمًا وَرُفَـٰتًا أَءِنَّا لَمَبْعُوثُونَ خَلْقًا جَدِيدًا

That is their compensation because they had disbelieved in Our miraculous signs. They had said: “When we are reduced to bones and dirt and decay, are we really going to be raised a new creation all over again?”

Ayah 99

أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ قَادِرٌ عَلَىٰٓ أَن يَخْلُقَ مِثْلَهُمْ وَجَعَلَ لَهُمْ أَجَلًا لَّا رَيْبَ فِيهِ فَأَبَى ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ إِلَّا كُفُورًا

Hadn’t they seen that Allah ﷻ, the One who created the skies and the earth, is capable of creating the likes of them? Didn’t they even think for a moment about Allah’s power of creation? First, that He’s created many nations before and destroyed many nations before—He has the power to create the likes of them. What do they think they are, that they’re so irreplaceable?

He’s given them not endless amounts of time to think about this. He made a deadline for them in which there is no doubt whatsoever. These wrongdoers, these misfits, they’re just bent upon disbelief. They will refuse anything but being disbelievers.

Ayah 100

قُل لَّوْ أَنتُمْ تَمْلِكُونَ خَزَآئِنَ رَحْمَةِ رَبِّىٓ إِذًا لَّأَمْسَكْتُمْ خَشْيَةَ ٱلْإِنفَاقِ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ قَتُورًا

Tell them in response to all their requests—a house of gold, a garden, waterfalls, bubbling water coming out of the ground, all these requests: If you in fact owned the treasures of the mercy of my Master, you would have stopped spending. You would have become cheap out of fear that you’ll have to spend and it will run out.

In other words, Allah ﷻ keeps giving His generosity to you. You’re asking for more. But let me tell you: if Allah ﷻ gave you what He has and you had the authority, you wouldn’t spend a dime of it. The human being is incredibly cheap and greedy.

Ayah 101

وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَىٰ تِسْعَ ءَايَـٰتٍۭ بَيِّنَـٰتٍ ۖ فَسْـَٔلْ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ إِذْ جَآءَهُمْ فَقَالَ لَهُۥ فِرْعَوْنُ إِنِّى لَأَظُنُّكَ يَـٰمُوسَىٰ مَسْحُورًا

We’re concluding with Musa (AS). Surah Al-Isra began with Musa (AS) and Firaun, and ends with Musa (AS) and Firaun. Notice specifically how the transition is going to happen.

We certainly had already given Musa (AS) nine miraculous signs. Go ask the Sons of Israel—when those signs came to them, when he came to them. Firaun said to him: “I’m pretty sure you are مَسْحُورًا —spellbound, a victim of magic. I think that’s what’s happened to you.”

Firaun’s final allegation against Musa (AS) is mashur. Firaun’s earlier allegation against Musa (AS) was sahir—a magician. Now he says: “You’re a victim of magic, not a perpetrator of it.”

Notice what’s not mentioned: Allah ﷻ gave him nine miraculous signs, and none of them worked. Why is this ayah not mentioning that? Look, many great signs have been given, but you are just like who? Just like Quraysh. Those signs didn’t help Firaun. He just said, “You’re infected by magic.”

What are you going to say? You’re not going to do any different. Here’s why I’m not sending you anything.

Ayah 102

قَالَ لَقَدْ عَلِمْتَ مَآ أَنزَلَ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ إِلَّا رَبُّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ بَصَآئِرَ وَإِنِّى لَأَظُنُّكَ يَـٰفِرْعَوْنُ مَثْبُورًا

Musa (AS) said: “You already know that nobody sent these miraculous signs down except the Master of the skies and the earth—بَصَآئِرَ —as things to clearly see.”

This is actually really powerful, heavy language from Musa (AS). “And I think—وَإِنِّى لَأَظُنُّكَ يَـٰفِرْعَوْنُ مَثْبُورًا —I think, O Firaun, you are mathbur.”

Mathbur comes from thubur. Thubur means death as a result of a poisonous injury—an injury that gets infected and you’re as good as dead. Then they say thabur. When the person sees Hellfire from a distance, they say mathbur.

He says: “I see the path you’ve taken—buddy, you’re as good as dead.” He just called it like it is. He called him out publicly: “I think, O Firaun, you are mathbur.”

Ayah 103–111 · linked video: 13. Al-Isra’ (Ayah 103-111)

Surah Al-Isra: Ayahs 103-111

Ayah 103

فَأَرَادَ أَن يَسْتَفِزَّهُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ فَأَغْرَقْنَـٰهُ وَمَن مَّعَهُۥ جَمِيعًا

So he intended to drive them from the land, but We drowned him and those with him all together.

Pharaoh’s ultimate plan was to expel Bani Israel from the land entirely - the word يَسْتَفِزَّهُم means to make someone slip or drive them out forcefully. This mirrors what we learned earlier in the Surah about those who wanted to drive the Prophet ﷺ out of Makkah. The parallel is striking and intentional.

But notice the response: Allah ﷻ says فَأَغْرَقْنَـٰهُ - “We drowned him.” Pharaoh might have felt secure on land, far from any body of water in the desert. Earlier in the Surah, Allah ﷻ asked: “Do you feel safe on land?” This false security was shattered when Allah ﷻ drowned Pharaoh and all his followers together. The punishment was complete and decisive - not just Pharaoh, but everyone who followed him in his tyranny.

Ayah 104

وَقُلْنَا مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ لِبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ ٱسْكُنُوا۟ ٱلْأَرْضَ فَإِذَا جَآءَ وَعْدُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ جِئْنَا بِكُمْ لَفِيفًا

And We said after him to the Children of Israel, “Dwell in the land, and when there comes the promise of the Hereafter, We will bring you forth in a mixed crowd.”

After Pharaoh’s destruction, Allah ﷻ commanded the Children of Israel to settle in the land. But the ayah contains a significant promise: فَإِذَا جَآءَ وَعْدُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ جِئْنَا بِكُمْ لَفِيفًا - “When the final promise arrives, We will bring you all together.”

The word لَفِيفًا literally means “folded together” in Arabic - it refers to different families and tribes from the same nation being gathered together from their scattered state. This is particularly significant because the Israelites experienced a diaspora lasting nearly two thousand years. They were exiled around 500 years before the Prophet ﷺ and remained scattered across the world.

According to hadith literature, Prophet Isa (AS) will return near the end of time and execute the law of Allah ﷻ that was long overdue. Modern scholars have noted an interesting phenomenon: since 1948, there has been a mass migration of Jewish people from all corners of the earth back to one land. Some scholars interpret this gathering as preparation for that final promise - when a nation must face divine judgment, having them gathered in one place rather than scattered across the globe becomes significant.

The traditional interpretation understands this gathering as occurring on the Day of Resurrection itself, when all humanity will be brought forth for judgment. Allah ﷻ knows best which understanding is correct, or if both contain truth.

However, we must understand something crucial here: the current inhabitants of Israel, whether they are believers or disbelievers, are recipients of the message of Islam. They are qualified to receive dawah. Any divine punishment that may come would not be because of what their ancestors did - that would be unjust. Rather, like all previous nations, punishment comes when a messenger arrives and people consciously reject the truth in the face of clear guidance.

Many among them may well come to believe. And importantly, the trials of the end times will be difficult for all nations, Muslims included. The believer and disbeliever alike will face tremendous tests. Anti-Semitism has no place in Islam. The Prophet ﷺ literally stood up when a Jewish funeral procession passed by. When the Companions (RA) asked him about it, reminding him it was a Jewish person, he responded, “Was it not a soul?” This is our Sunnah - respect and dignity for all human beings.

The Quran teaches us to have respect for the Jewish community, if for no other reason than because they are Children of Ibrahim (AS) and Ishaq (AS). They are a people from whom many prophets arose - more prophets are mentioned from their lineage than any other nation in the Quran. When the Companions (RA) were told about role models, they were told about the companions of previous prophets from Bani Israel.

The word “Israel” itself - which has unfortunately become politically charged - simply means عَبْدُ الله, servant of Allah. It’s the same in Arabic and Hebrew. It’s not a negative term; it’s actually the name of Prophet Yaqub (AS), a noble prophet of Allah ﷻ.

We cannot allow the political climate of our times to cloud our religious understanding. Yes, our religion says things that will offend people - that happened during the Prophet’s ﷺ time too. When he told them about Al-Isra and Al-Miraj, they mocked him. But he didn’t feel the need to justify the miracle with scientific explanations. He simply affirmed his belief. That’s his iman, and that’s sufficient.

We need to develop a sound understanding of our religion. When we have half-baked understandings from incomplete lectures and talks, especially about Jews or other communities, our youth suffer. They have Jewish friends at school, and when they hear inflammatory rhetoric at home or in the masjid, they start questioning: “Why does my religion want me to hate people so much?” This leads them to question Islam itself - even though what they heard was a terrible misrepresentation of Islam.

Allah ﷻ did not turn all Jews into monkeys and pigs. Those who specifically mocked Allah’s revelation received that divine punishment. And are we immune from such punishments if we mock revelation? Absolutely not.

Honestly speaking - and this is my personal reflection - the state of moral, ethical and religious decline that our Ummah is in today is such that if the worst Jews from history were alive, they might look at us and say, “We thought we were bad, but you people are something else. At least we could change our book and then mess up. You can’t even change yours, and you’re still this messed up.”

If you sit down and honestly examine the average Muslim community today, whether abroad or here, there’s plenty of reason for concern. We’re not the righteous Sahaba (RA). Before we look critically at anybody else, we need to look at ourselves first. Only when we’re done examining ourselves can we look elsewhere.

One of the great mistakes we make is looking only at the glorious moments of our history - the Muslim empires, the expansions, the inventions, the golden age of Islamic civilization. We write articles about how great we were and how Western civilization owes us thanks. But that wasn’t us - those were our ancestors who already died. They’ve answered to Allah ﷻ for what they did.

What have we done? The Muslim world hasn’t produced so much as a ballpoint pen in recent times. What have we given the world? We’re not in a position to criticize the rest of the world. When people cite Islamic history, they mention the great warriors and scholars. But were they the only ones? No. We need a complete, honest understanding of our entire history - including the mistakes - because if you don’t understand what mistakes were made in the past, you’re doomed to repeat them.

Being delusional about your past is precisely the Bani Israel problem described in the Quran. They thought they were the preferred people, that the world was against them, that Allah ﷻ would help them just because they had this awesome ancestry. That’s not how it works. It never works that way.

We need to take an honest look at our history and stop blaming everyone else - “the Jews are doing this, America is doing this, the United Nations, the Europeans, these sanctions, that policy.” What are you doing? What are we doing? We waste so many opportunities just sitting and complaining about what everybody else is up to.

Imagine: the Dajjal appears. What do you do? Do you stop everything to obsess over what he’s doing - “Oh my God, he’s got this donkey with an enormous wingspan, he can make rain appear, he brought this dead person back to life”? No. The Prophet ﷺ taught us: if you’re in the middle of planting a seed, finish planting it. Do your work. You have responsibilities to fulfill.

We should be aware of what’s happening politically, but we shouldn’t be overwhelmed by it. Elections, political dramas - these things come and go. They’re fluff in the grand scheme. Be aware of your society, but don’t be consumed by it. What should overwhelm us is the need to produce good work, to show the beauty of Islam to the world. That takes dedication and diligence on our part.

We’ll keep getting distracted if we focus only on things beyond our control. Let’s focus on what we can actually do.

Ayah 105

وَبِٱلْحَقِّ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ وَبِٱلْحَقِّ نَزَلَ ۗ وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ إِلَّا مُبَشِّرًا وَنَذِيرًا

And with truth We have sent it down, and with truth it has descended. And We have not sent you, [O Muhammad], except as a bringer of good tidings and a warner.

This ayah emphasizes two dimensions of the Quran’s revelation. وَبِٱلْحَقِّ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ - “With truth We have sent it down” - the commandment was issued that this Book would come down with divine purpose. وَبِٱلْحَقِّ نَزَلَ - “And with truth it descended” - the entire delivery was done purposefully and correctly, with absolute precision.

Even at the end of this passage about Bani Israel, Allah ﷻ is still extending mercy and leaving the door open. The Prophet ﷺ is sent only as a bearer of good news and a warner - not as a judge or executioner. The invitation to guidance remains open to all.

Ayah 106

وَقُرْءَانًا فَرَقْنَـٰهُ لِتَقْرَأَهُۥ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ عَلَىٰ مُكْثٍ وَنَزَّلْنَـٰهُ تَنزِيلًا

And [it is] a Quran which We have separated [by intervals] that you might recite it to the people over a prolonged period. And We have sent it down progressively.

This incredible Quran, this incredible recitation - فَرَقْنَـٰهُ means We have distinguished it, made it part by part. Why? لِتَقْرَأَهُۥ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ عَلَىٰ مُكْثٍ - so that you may recite it to the people with مُكْث.

The word mukth has layers of meaning. It means to wait for something, to remain in anticipation of something. It also means to recite something slowly and clearly. The Prophet ﷺ would recite slowly and clearly to the people, and the revelation would come on the right occasion when that guidance was needed. This is a tremendous mercy from Allah ﷻ.

Imagine if the entire Quran had been revealed at once. When the Companions (RA) needed guidance on a specific issue, they would have to search through the entire revelation - and they didn’t have written copies initially, everything was memorized. They would have to recite from the beginning to find the relevant ayah. Instead, Allah ﷻ revealed guidance precisely when it was needed: “You need to deal with this issue? Here’s the ayah. That issue? Here’s the ayah.”

وَنَزَّلْنَـٰهُ تَنزِيلًا - “And We sent it down over a long period of time” - gradually, purposefully, with perfect timing.

Ayah 107

قُلْ ءَامِنُوا۟ بِهِۦٓ أَوْ لَا تُؤْمِنُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْعِلْمَ مِن قَبْلِهِۦٓ إِذَا يُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ يَخِرُّونَ لِلْأَذْقَانِ سُجَّدًا

Say, “Believe in it or do not believe. Indeed, those who were given knowledge before it - when it is recited to them, they fall upon their faces in prostration.”

Allah ﷻ tells His Messenger ﷺ to say: believe it or don’t believe it. The truth doesn’t need your validation. But look at those who truly have knowledge - إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْعِلْمَ مِن قَبْلِهِۦٓ - those who were given knowledge long before this revelation arrived.

These were the rabbis of Bani Israel who had been waiting for this revelation. They had been sitting in Madinah, generation after generation, saying, “It’s coming, it’s coming.” When the Prophet ﷺ arrived and recited Quran to them, إِذَا يُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ يَخِرُّونَ لِلْأَذْقَانِ سُجَّدًا - they fall upon their chins in prostration.

The mention of the chin is significant. When you prostrate normally, your forehead touches the ground first. But the chin is mentioned because they lost complete control over their limbs. They fell so hard, so overwhelmed by what they were hearing, that they couldn’t help themselves. They collapsed.

Imagine their emotion: “Is this really happening? I’m alive to witness this? My great-great-great-grandfather told his son, who told his son, who told his son, and we’ve been waiting for generations. Is it happening in my lifetime? My children will get to see the Messenger of Allah ﷻ?”

Ayah 108

وَيَقُولُونَ سُبْحَـٰنَ رَبِّنَآ إِن كَانَ وَعْدُ رَبِّنَا لَمَفْعُولًا

And they say, “Exalted is our Lord! Indeed, the promise of our Lord has been fulfilled.”

Their response upon hearing the Quran: سُبْحَـٰنَ رَبِّنَآ - How perfect is our Master! إِن كَانَ وَعْدُ رَبِّنَا لَمَفْعُولًا - The promise of our Lord has absolutely been fulfilled. The emphasis with إِن and the lam in لَمَفْعُولًا expresses their awe and certainty. Right here, right now, in this moment, the ancient promise has come true.

Ayah 109

وَيَخِرُّونَ لِلْأَذْقَانِ يَبْكُونَ وَيَزِيدُهُمْ خُشُوعًا ۩

And they fall upon their faces weeping, and it increases them in humble submission.

وَيَخِرُّونَ لِلْأَذْقَانِ يَبْكُونَ - They fall upon their chins weeping. What does the recitation of Quran do to them? وَيَزِيدُهُمْ خُشُوعًا - It increases them in more and more humility and submission before Allah ﷻ. This is an ayah of sajdah, where prostration is recommended.

There’s a beautiful narration: a group of people came to Umar (RA) and asked to hear the Quran. When they heard it, they began to cry. Umar (RA) said, “That’s how we used to be, until our hearts became hard.” The ability to cry when the Quran is recited is a gift from Allah ﷻ. It doesn’t come to everyone, and it doesn’t come all the time. But if your heart is in the right place, your intention is sincere, and you understand what you’re reciting, you won’t be able to help yourself. It will happen.

The best time for this experience is in Salah, especially in the night prayer, when you’re alone with your Lord and His words.

Ayah 110

قُلِ ٱدْعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ أَوِ ٱدْعُوا۟ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنَ ۖ أَيًّا مَّا تَدْعُوا۟ فَلَهُ ٱلْأَسْمَآءُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۚ وَلَا تَجْهَرْ بِصَلَاتِكَ وَلَا تُخَافِتْ بِهَا وَٱبْتَغِ بَيْنَ ذَٰلِكَ سَبِيلًا

Say, “Call upon Allah or call upon the Most Merciful. Whichever [name] you call - to Him belong the best names.” And do not recite [too] loudly in your prayer or [too] quietly but seek between that an [intermediate] way.

قُلِ ٱدْعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ أَوِ ٱدْعُوا۟ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنَ - Say: Call upon Allah, or call upon Ar-Rahman. أَيًّا مَّا تَدْعُوا۟ فَلَهُ ٱلْأَسْمَآءُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ - Whichever you call, to Him belong the Most Beautiful Names exclusively.

Then comes practical guidance: وَلَا تَجْهَرْ بِصَلَاتِكَ وَلَا تُخَافِتْ بِهَا - Don’t be too loud when you pray, and don’t be too quiet either. Some Companions used to recite so loudly it could be heard from far away; others would recite so softly you could barely hear it. The Prophet ﷺ guided them: lower your voice a bit, raise yours a bit. وَٱبْتَغِ بَيْنَ ذَٰلِكَ سَبِيلًا - Find a balanced path between these two extremes.

This teaches us the Islamic principle of moderation - not just in prayer, but in all aspects of life.

Ayah 111

وَقُلِ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِى لَمْ يَتَّخِذْ وَلَدًا وَلَمْ يَكُن لَّهُۥ شَرِيكٌ فِى ٱلْمُلْكِ وَلَمْ يَكُن لَّهُۥ وَلِىٌّ مِّنَ ٱلذُّلِّ ۖ وَكَبِّرْهُ تَكْبِيرًۢا

And say, “Praise to Allah, who has not taken a son and has had no partner in [His] dominion and has no [need of a] protector out of weakness; and glorify Him with [great] glorification.”

This final ayah is a gift of Tawheed - pure monotheism. It contains five fundamental teachings.

First: وَقُلِ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ - Say: All praise belongs to Allah ﷻ.

Second: ٱلَّذِى لَمْ يَتَّخِذْ وَلَدًا - The One who has not taken a son. This directly addresses Christian beliefs.

Third: وَلَمْ يَكُن لَّهُۥ شَرِيكٌ فِى ٱلْمُلْكِ - He has no partner in His dominion. This addresses the Makkan mushrikeen and their idolatry.

Fourth: وَلَمْ يَكُن لَّهُۥ وَلِىٌّ مِّنَ ٱلذُّلِّ - He has never had any protector out of weakness. Sometimes people maintain friendships because they’re weak and need connections - they can’t afford to make someone angry because that person might be useful later. Allah ﷻ declares He has never had any such relationship, because He has no weakness whatsoever.

Fifth: وَكَبِّرْهُ تَكْبِيرًۢا - Declare His greatness with all glorification. Proclaim that He is greater than anything that could be imagined or compared to Him.

Notice something profound: this ayah’s conclusion is directly tied to the beginning of the Surah. The Surah began with سُبْحَانَ - exalted is He. It ends with الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ - all praise belongs to Allah. The middle portion addresses divine authority and kingdom - الْمُلْكِ.

These three concepts together - سُبْحَانَ اللَّهِ, الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ, and اللَّهُ أَكْبَرُ - form the common phrase the Prophet ﷺ used to recite regularly. The beginning and end of this beautiful Surah capture the essence of these remembrances.

There’s one more critical point about وَكَبِّرْهُ تَكْبِيرًۢا and the theme of Tawheed in this Surah. There are two kinds of shirk that this Surah addresses.

The first kind is elevating creation - taking created beings and saying things about them that are so high, you’re essentially raising them to divine status. This is the Christian error.

The second kind is reducing Allah’s status - saying things about Allah ﷻ that shouldn’t be said, bringing Him down from His true magnificence. This is the Jewish error, as when they said, “Allah’s hand is tied” (meaning He is stingy), or when they questioned His power and wisdom.

Which audience was primarily addressed in Surah Al-Isra? The Jewish community. Therefore, they are taught throughout not to commit the shirk of reducing Allah’s status. From the very beginning - سُبْحَانَ - How perfect He is, you cannot bring Him down. الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِى لَمْ يَتَّخِذْ وَلَدًا - He didn’t take a son. He has no partners because of any weakness. And declare His absolute greatness - وَكَبِّرْهُ تَكْبِيرًۢا.

The next Surah, Surah Al-Kahf, will teach another lesson of Tawheed - but it will address the other side. It will teach us not to take creation and elevate it too high. The Christians and their beliefs about Isa (AS) will be addressed there, showing the other dimension of pure Tawheed.

This is the beautiful balance and perfect structure of the Quran - each Surah complementing the others, together forming a complete guidance for humanity.
